title: --mcp flag is a no-op: not wired through to Claude CLI or OpenCode backends
description: The --mcp flag is accepted by orch spawn and documented in help text, but never passed through to either backend. For Claude Code: needs to generate MCP config JSON and pass via claude --mcp-config. For OpenCode: MCP is server-level config, so either document this limitation or find a per-session mechanism.
repro: 1. orch spawn --bypass-triage --mcp playwright --backend claude --no-track --tmux hello 'test task' 2. Agent starts but has NO playwright MCP tools available 3. ps aux shows Claude CLI launched without --mcp-config flag

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix: orch spawn --mcp flag is accepted but never wired through to either backend.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: When spawning agents that need browser automation (Playwright MCP), the --mcp flag silently does nothing. This means agents can't use Playwright even when explicitly requested.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Medium

## Problem
The --mcp flag on orch spawn is documented in help text and accepted without error, but:
1. For Claude Code backend: the spawned 'claude' CLI process has NO --mcp-config flag passed
2. For OpenCode backend: MCP is server-level config, unclear if per-session injection is possible

## Evidence
- Spawned: orch spawn --bypass-triage --mcp playwright --backend claude --no-track --tmux hello 'test'
- Agent reported: 'Playwright MCP tools NOT AVAILABLE'
- ps aux shows: claude --dangerously-skip-permissions (no --mcp-config)

## Fix Required (Claude Code backend)
1. Find where the --mcp flag value is stored in SpawnConfig
2. Find where the Claude CLI command is constructed (likely in tmux spawn or cmd/orch/ spawn code)
3. When --mcp is set, generate appropriate MCP config and pass it via: claude --mcp-config <config>
4. Claude CLI accepts: --mcp-config <configs...> (JSON files or inline JSON strings, space-separated)

## For OpenCode backend
Investigate whether OpenCode API supports per-session MCP config. If not, document this as a known limitation (MCP only works with --backend claude).

## Scope
IN: Wire --mcp flag through to Claude CLI --mcp-config for claude backend. Handle at minimum 'playwright' as a known MCP preset.
OUT: Adding new MCP server presets beyond playwright. Overhauling MCP architecture.

## Verification
After fix: orch spawn --bypass-triage --mcp playwright --backend claude --no-track --tmux hello 'list MCP servers'
Expected: Agent should report mcp__playwright__* tools are available.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Reading spawn code to trace --mcp flag flow

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Wired --mcp flag through to Claude CLI --mcp-config. Added MCPConfigJSON() preset resolver (playwright → JSON config), BuildClaudeLaunchCommand() for testable command construction, and MCP in event data/summary for Claude backend. Tests: go test ./pkg/spawn/ -run 'TestMCPConfigJSON|TestBuildClaudeLaunchCommand' - 6 passed, 0 failed. Smoke test requires manual execution (nested Claude Code restriction). No discovered work.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
The --mcp flag was silently ignored — Claude CLI was launched without --mcp-config. Fix adds preset resolution (playwright → npx @playwright/mcp config JSON) and wires it into the CLI command. Verified: spawned hello agent with --mcp playwright now gets all 22 mcp__playwright__* tools. OpenCode backend documented as unsupported (server-level config only).

title: Wire --mcp flag through to OpenCode backend via opencode.json
description: The --mcp flag now works for Claude Code backend (orch-go-1123). For OpenCode backend, MCP servers are configured in opencode.json under the 'mcp' key. When --mcp playwright is passed with --backend opencode, orch should write/merge the playwright entry into the target project's opencode.json before creating the session. Format: {"mcp":{"playwright":{"type":"local","command":["npx","-y","@playwright/mcp@latest"],"enabled":true}}}. Reference: ~/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch/opencode.json for working example.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Wire --mcp flag through to OpenCode backend by writing MCP config into opencode.json.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: The --mcp flag now works for Claude Code backend (orch-go-1123 just shipped). But OpenCode backend silently ignores it. OpenCode uses opencode.json for MCP config — we need to write/merge the MCP preset into that file before spawning the session.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Context
- orch-go-1123 added MCPConfigJSON() in pkg/spawn/claude.go with a mcpPresets map
- OpenCode MCP config format (from ~/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch/opencode.json):
  {"mcp":{"playwright":{"type":"local","command":["npx","-y","@playwright/mcp@latest"],"enabled":true}}}
- The opencode.json lives in the target project directory (cfg.WorkDir or project root)
- OpenCode spawn logic is in pkg/spawn/ (likely opencode.go or similar)

## Implementation
1. Add an OpenCode MCP preset format (different from Claude's format — needs 'type' and 'enabled' fields)
2. Before spawning an OpenCode session, if cfg.MCP is set:
   a. Read existing opencode.json (or create if missing)
   b. Merge the MCP preset into the 'mcp' key
   c. Write it back
3. Reuse the mcpPresets map concept from claude.go but with OpenCode-format JSON
4. Consider cleanup: should the injected MCP entry be removed after session ends? Probably not — leave it.

## Scope
IN: Wire --mcp through to opencode.json for OpenCode backend spawns
OUT: Cleanup/removal of injected config, new MCP presets beyond playwright

## Verification
1. go test ./pkg/spawn/ passes
2. go build ./cmd/orch/ && go vet ./cmd/orch/ passes
3. Manual: orch spawn --bypass-triage --mcp playwright --backend opencode --no-track hello 'list MCP tools' should show playwright tools

title: OpenCode MCP hot-reload: server ignores opencode.json changes after startup
description: orch-go-1125 wires --mcp into opencode.json before spawning, but OpenCode loads MCP config at server init, not per-session. The written config is correct but the running server never picks it up. Fix options: (1) Add hot-reload API endpoint in OpenCode fork to reload MCP servers from config, (2) Add file watcher on opencode.json for automatic reload, (3) Restart server after config change (disruptive). This is an OpenCode fork change (~/Documents/personal/opencode), not orch-go. The orch-go side (writing opencode.json) already works.
repro: 1. Start OpenCode server (orch-dashboard start) 2. orch spawn --mcp playwright --backend opencode --model codex --no-track hello 'list MCP tools' 3. opencode.json is correctly written with playwright config 4. Agent reports 0 mcp__playwright tools — server loaded config at startup, ignores runtime changes

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: OpenCode server ignores opencode.json changes after startup. MCP servers are loaded once at init and never reloaded.

TASK: Add a file watcher on opencode.json that triggers MCP server reload when the config changes. This is the simplest reliable approach — no API endpoint needed, no server restart.

CONTEXT: orch-go writes opencode.json with --mcp config before spawning agents. The file is written correctly but the running server never picks up changes.

SCOPE:
- IN: File watcher on opencode.json, MCP server reload on change
- OUT: API endpoints, server restart logic, orch-go changes

VERIFICATION: 
1. Start server
2. Modify opencode.json to add an MCP server
3. Verify server picks up the change without restart

title: --backend claude should auto-resolve to Anthropic model when project defaults to OpenAI
description: When a project's default model is OpenAI (e.g. price-watch), explicitly passing --backend claude should auto-resolve the model to an Anthropic one (default: sonnet, or opus if --model opus). The backend flag is an explicit intent signal — the user is saying 'use Claude CLI' which inherently means Anthropic. Currently orch tries to use the project's OpenAI default with Claude CLI, which is impossible. Fix: when --backend claude is explicit and resolved model is OpenAI, override to default Anthropic model (sonnet).
repro: 1. In price-watch project (defaults to OpenAI model via opencode config) 2. orch spawn feature-impl task --backend claude --bypass-triage 3. Error: backend claude does not support provider openai 4. Expected: --backend claude should auto-select sonnet/opus regardless of project model default

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix: --backend claude should auto-resolve to Anthropic model when project defaults to OpenAI.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: In cross-project spawns (e.g. price-watch which defaults to OpenAI), passing --backend claude fails with 'backend claude does not support provider openai'. The user explicitly chose Claude CLI — that inherently means Anthropic. The model resolution should detect this and auto-select an Anthropic model (default sonnet).

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## Problem
When a project's default model is OpenAI and the user passes --backend claude:
1. orch resolves the model to the project default (OpenAI)
2. Then checks backend compatibility
3. Fails: 'backend claude does not support provider openai'

## Expected
--backend claude + no explicit --model → auto-resolve to anthropic/claude-sonnet (or the Anthropic default)
--backend claude + --model opus → works (already does since model is explicit)

## Where to Look
- Model/backend resolution logic — likely in cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go or pkg/spawn/config.go or pkg/model/
- Recent commits touched this area: 'feat: wire resolved spawn settings', 'fix: respect explicit spawn_mode only'
- The guard that produces the error message is the right place to add fallback logic

## Fix
When --backend claude is explicit and the resolved model provider is not anthropic:
1. Override model to default Anthropic model (anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5-20250514 or whatever the current default is)
2. Log a warning: 'Auto-resolved model to sonnet (claude backend requires Anthropic model)'

## Scope
IN: Auto-resolve model when backend is explicitly claude and model conflicts
OUT: Changing default model resolution for other cases

## Verification
go test ./cmd/orch/ ./pkg/spawn/ ./pkg/model/ passes
go build ./cmd/orch/ && go vet ./cmd/orch/ passes

title: Audit price-watch spawn prompts: quality, noise, missing context, sizing
description: Audit workspaces in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch/.orch/workspace from the last day. Focus on SPAWN_CONTEXT.md files across all recent workspaces. Evaluate: (1) What's off or doesn't make sense in the prompts? (2) What shouldn't be there (noise, irrelevant context, wasted tokens)? (3) What SHOULD be there but is missing? (4) How's the sizing — too long, too short, right-sized? Deliverable: structured audit with specific examples and recommendations.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Audit spawn prompt quality across recent workspaces in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch/.orch/workspace (last 24 hours only).

For each workspace's SPAWN_CONTEXT.md, evaluate:

1. WHAT'S OFF — Anything confusing, contradictory, or that would mislead an agent
2. WHAT SHOULDN'T BE THERE — Noise, irrelevant context, template bloat, wasted tokens (e.g. ORIENTATION_FRAME that workers don't need)
3. WHAT'S MISSING — Context the agent needs but wasn't given (project-specific conventions, relevant prior art, clear scope boundaries, verification commands)
4. SIZING — Is each prompt right-sized for the task? Too verbose? Too terse? Does complexity match the task scope?
5. PROMPT PATTERNS — What patterns recur across prompts? What's working vs what's cargo-culted?

Compare against what a well-crafted spawn prompt looks like: clear task, scope boundaries (IN/OUT), specific files/lines when possible, verification command, prior art references.

DELIVERABLE: Investigation at .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-inv-price-watch-spawn-prompt-audit.md with specific examples (quote the problematic sections), a pattern summary, and actionable recommendations.

title: Progressive skill disclosure - inject only relevant phases/modes
description: Full skill documents (400-1,000 lines) are inlined into every spawn prompt regardless of which phases/modes the task uses. Design needed: inject compact summary + only specific phase/mode guidance needed. Biggest token savings (2,000-6,000 tokens/prompt) but needs architect pass first. Evidence: .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-inv-price-watch-spawn-prompt-audit.md Finding 3, Recommendation P3

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design progressive skill disclosure for spawn prompts. Currently full skill documents (400-1,000 lines) are inlined into every SPAWN_CONTEXT.md regardless of which phases/modes the task uses. A feature-impl spawn with --phases implementation,validation gets all 6 phases and 3 modes (530+ lines). Worker-base inlines constitutional limits (100+ lines), cross-repo handoff (40+ lines), and visual debugging tools — all rarely needed.

DESIGN GOAL: Inject only what the agent needs for THIS spawn. Estimated savings: 2,000-6,000 tokens per prompt (from ~17k to ~11-15k).

DESIGN CONSTRAINTS:
- Skills are currently loaded as full markdown files by pkg/skills/loader.go
- Spawn context is generated by pkg/spawn/context.go using Go templates
- Skills use ## headings to structure sections (phases, modes, authority, etc.)
- The --phases and --mode flags already exist on orch spawn
- skillc compiler at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/ compiles skills from .skillc directories
- Worker-base is a dependency skill loaded alongside the primary skill

DESIGN QUESTIONS TO ANSWER:
1. How should skills declare which sections are phase/mode-specific vs always-included?
2. Should section filtering happen at skill load time (loader.go) or template render time (context.go)?
3. How should worker-base handle its rarely-needed sections (constitutional limits, cross-repo handoff)?
4. What metadata format lets skillc compile section markers without breaking existing skill content?

DELIVERABLE: Architecture recommendation with specific implementation plan. Include section format proposal, filtering algorithm, and migration path for existing skills. Reference: .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-inv-price-watch-spawn-prompt-audit.md for the evidence.

title: Daemon sets beads assignee but never spawns OpenCode session
description: Daemon is polling bd ready and setting assignees on issues, but failing silently before creating OpenCode sessions or workspaces. Result: issues appear claimed but no agent is actually working. Phantom assignees block daemon from re-processing the issue. Log at ~/.orch/logs/orch-2026-02.log shows only [status] entries, zero spawn events for the entire month. Two daemon processes running manually (no launchd service). Affected issues: orch-go-1135 through 1138, 1141.
repro: Created 5 issues with triage:ready label. Daemon set assignees (og-arch-dedup-orientation-frame-20feb-5de7 etc) on all 5 within 2 min, but: zero workspaces created, zero OpenCode sessions, zero spawn events in ~/.orch/logs/orch-2026-02.log (entire log has 0 'spawn' entries). Two daemon processes running (PIDs 96434, 62329) but log shows only [status] polls, no spawn activity.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Daemon sets beads assignee but never spawns OpenCode session. Two daemon processes running (PIDs visible in ps aux), log at ~/.orch/logs/orch-2026-02.log shows ONLY [status] polls, zero spawn events for the entire month. Daemon code is in pkg/daemon/daemon.go and cmd/orch/daemon.go. The daemon appears to: (1) poll bd ready, (2) infer skill, (3) set assignee on beads issue, then (4) FAIL SILENTLY before creating OpenCode session or workspace. Find where the spawn fails and fix it. Verification: after fix, orch daemon preview shows a spawnable issue, orch daemon once actually creates an OpenCode session and workspace.

title: Implement progressive skill disclosure (section filtering)

Four phases: (1) Add FilterSkillSections() and SectionFilter to pkg/skills/loader.go, (2) Wire into spawn_cmd.go, (3) Add @section markers to skill sources in ~/orch-knowledge, (4) Verify 20%+ token reduction. comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Implement progressive skill disclosure — section filtering for spawn prompts. Design approved at .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-design-progressive-skill-disclosure.md.

PHASE 1: Add FilterSkillSections() and SectionFilter to pkg/skills/loader.go
- SectionFilter struct with Phases []string, Mode string, SpawnMode string
- FilterSkillSections(content string, filter *SectionFilter) string — scans for <!-- @section: key=value --> markers, strips non-matching sections, preserves unmarked content
- LoadSkillFiltered(skillName string, filter *SectionFilter) — loads skill with deps then filters
- Tests in pkg/skills/loader_test.go: nil filter unchanged, phase filtering, mode filtering, malformed markers passthrough

PHASE 2: Wire into spawn_cmd.go where skills are loaded
- Build SectionFilter from --phases and --mode flags
- Call LoadSkillFiltered instead of LoadSkillWithDependencies
- Backward compatible: empty flags = no filtering

PHASE 3: Add @section markers to skill sources in ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/
- feature-impl: wrap each phase (investigation, clarifying-questions, design, implementation x3 modes, validation, integration) with <!-- @section: phase=X --> / <!-- @/section -->
- architect: wrap autonomous/interactive mode sections with <!-- @section: spawn-mode=X -->
- Run skillc deploy after adding markers

PHASE 4: Verify
- go test ./pkg/skills/... passes
- Generate SPAWN_CONTEXT.md with --phases implementation,validation --mode tdd and confirm 20%+ fewer skill tokens vs unfiltered

DO NOT filter worker-base constitutional sections (intentional design choice — 600 tokens not worth complexity).

title: Cross-repo spawns: deliverable path should follow the model owner, not the workdir
description:
When orchestrator spawns a probe with --workdir pointing to repo A (source code) but the model being updated lives in repo B (consumer), the artifact lands in repo A's .kb/ and never gets integrated into repo B's model.

Fix options:
1. Spawn prompt linting: if DELIVERABLE path and --workdir are in different repos, warn the orchestrator
2. Explicit deliverable_repo field in spawn context that overrides where artifacts are written
3. Post-completion check: if probe references a model in a different repo, flag for manual integration

Reference: kb-650292 (constraint in price-watch)
repro: 1. Orchestrator spawns probe with --workdir ~/sendcutsend to investigate SCS source code. 2. Probe deliverable lands in sendcutsend/.kb/investigations/. 3. The model being tested (scs-api-surface.md) lives in price-watch/.kb/models/. 4. Knowledge never gets integrated — sat disconnected for a week. 5. Team rebuilt the answer from scratch (pw-9018 cart API) before rediscovering the existing investigation.

title: Audit verification infrastructure: what exists, what works, where the gaps are

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: We've built various verification features across orch-go (verify package, completion gates, behavioral checks, test evidence detection, etc.) but have no clear picture of what actually works end-to-end vs what's enforcement theater. Before making decisions about the verification vocabulary gap (how to communicate 'what level of verification is needed'), we need to know what exists.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Medium

AUDIT DIMENSION: architecture (focused on verification infrastructure)

WHAT TO AUDIT:
1. pkg/verify/ - What checks exist? What do they actually verify? Are they wired into real flows?
2. Completion verification in complete_cmd.go / complete_pipeline.go - What behavioral gates fire? What evidence do they check?
3. Daemon verification - Does the daemon check anything before auto-closing? Is IsPaused/VerificationTracker wired?
4. Test evidence detection (pkg/verify/test_evidence.go) - What does it detect? Is it used?
5. Any --dry-run, --preview, or smoke-test capabilities across commands
6. The coaching plugin's verification-related detections (if any)

FOR EACH FEATURE FOUND:
- Does it exist as code? (implementation)
- Is it wired into a real flow? (integration)

title: Design verification levels: vocabulary gap + anti-proliferation
description:
Design explicit verification levels that solve the orchestrator-human vocabulary gap ('what kind of verification does this need?') without repeating the gate proliferation failure (14 gates → flag combos → --force as default).

Three constraints from Dylan:
1. Verification level should be explicit per-spawn (declared) AND inferrable from work type (default). The level determines which gates fire — inverse of current 'all gates on, skip what doesn't apply.'
2. --explain and --verify should be orchestrator judgment calls, not mechanical checkboxes. The orchestrator decides 'I need to verify this behaviorally' as part of completion reasoning, not because a flag forced it.
3. Gate friction must stay below the --force threshold. We've lived through the failure mode: gates proliferate, each reasonable, aggregate friction makes --force the happy path, system collapses to theater.

Inputs:
- Audit: .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-audit-verification-infrastructure-end-to-end.md (14 gates inventory, spectrum gap analysis)
- Models: ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/verifiability-first-development.md, ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/control-plane-bootstrap.md
- Anti-pattern evidence: completion gate proliferation history (ask about Phase 6→7 gate removal)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan and the orchestrator can't communicate about verification. 'Verify this works' could mean 5 different things. We built 14 completion gates to address this but they proliferated into a checklist that required --force to bypass. We need verification LEVELS — a shared vocabulary where the orchestrator declares what kind of verification a change needs, and only the relevant checks fire.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Medium

DESIGN QUESTION: How should verification levels work in the orch completion system?

THREE HARD CONSTRAINTS (from Dylan):
1. LEVELS OVER GATES: Verification level declared per-spawn determines which gates fire. Inverse of current 'all on, skip what doesn't apply.' A config change should not trigger test execution evidence checks. A daemon bug fix should.
2. JUDGMENT OVER MECHANICS: --explain and --verify should be orchestrator judgment calls during completion reasoning, not mechanical flag requirements. The orchestrator decides 'I need to verify this behaviorally' — the flag records that decision, doesn't trigger it.
3. FRICTION CEILING: Gate friction must stay below the --force threshold. Historical failure mode: gates proliferate → flag combos needed → --force becomes default → theater. Any design that makes --force the happy path has failed.

REQUIRED INPUTS (read these):
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-audit-verification-infrastructure-end-to-end.md (14 gates inventory, spectrum gap, anti-theater mechanisms)
- ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/verifiability-first-development.md (verification paradigm)
- ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/control-plane-bootstrap.md (enforcement theater)
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go and cmd/orch/complete_pipeline.go (current completion flow)
- cmd/orch/complete_verify.go (current SkipConfig / gate bypass system)
- pkg/verify/check.go (current 14-gate implementation)

WHAT TO DESIGN:
1. A verification level taxonomy (e.g., L0-L4) that both human and orchestrator understand
2. How levels map to existing gates (which gates fire at which level)
3. How levels are declared (at spawn time? inferred from skill/work-type? overridable?)
4. How this replaces the current SkipConfig/flag-combo system
5. How --explain and --verify become judgment-driven rather than mechanical

WHAT TO AVOID:
- Adding MORE gates (we have 14, that's enough)
- Any design where the common case requires flags to pass completion
- Designs that remove orchestrator judgment in favor of automation
- Over-engineering — the simplest design that solves the vocabulary gap wins

DELIVERABLE: Investigation with recommendations at .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-inv-architect-verification-levels.md

IN SCOPE: Design and recommendation
OUT OF SCOPE: Implementation

We need verification LEVELS — a shared vocabulary where the orchestrator declares what kind of verification a change needs, and only the relevant checks fire.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Medium

DESIGN QUESTION: How should verification levels work in the orch completion system?

THREE HARD CONSTRAINTS (from Dylan):
1. LEVELS OVER GATES: Verification level declared per-spawn determines which gates fire. Inverse of current 'all on, skip what doesn't apply.' A config change should not trigger test execution evidence checks. A daemon bug fix should.
2. JUDGMENT OVER MECHANICS: --explain and --verify should be orchestrator judgment calls during completion reasoning, not mechanical flag requirements. The orchestrator decides 'I need to verify this behaviorally' — the flag records that decision, doesn't trigger it.
3. FRICTION CEILING: Gate friction must stay below the --force threshold. Historical failure mode: gates proliferate → flag combos needed → --force becomes default → theater. Any design that makes --force the happy path has failed.

CRITICAL CONTEXT FROM PARALLEL WORK (orch-go-1158):
A companion investigation on tradeoff visibility concluded that:
- Tradeoff surfacing belongs UPSTREAM (at spawn/triage time), not downstream (at completion)
- Model 'Pressure Points' sections should document architectural fragility
- The orchestrator should check pressure points at triage time as a judgment call
- Completion-time gates should be a safety net, not the primary mechanism
- Adding more required sections to SYNTHESIS.md and more completion gates is EXACTLY the anti-pattern Dylan warned about

THIS DESIGN MUST INTEGRATE WITH THAT CONCLUSION:
- Verification levels should be the framework that SUBSUMES both verification gates AND tradeoff visibility
- The orchestrator declares a verification level at spawn time based on judgment (informed by pressure points, work type, risk)
- The level determines what's checked at completion — not a proliferating list of independent gates
- Tradeoff declaration (Architectural Choices in SYNTHESIS.md) should be part of higher verification levels, not a universal gate

REQUIRED INPUTS (read these):
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-audit-verification-infrastructure-end-to-end.md (14 gates inventory, spectrum gap)
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-design-tradeoff-visibility-for-non-code-reading-orchestrator.md (tradeoff visibility design — integrate, don't duplicate)
- ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/verifiability-first-development.md (verification paradigm)
- ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/control-plane-bootstrap.md (enforcement theater)
- cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go and cmd/orch/complete_pipeline.go (current completion flow)
- cmd/orch/complete_verify.go (current SkipConfig / gate bypass system)
- pkg/verify/check.go (current 14-gate implementation)

WHAT TO DESIGN:
1. A verification level taxonomy (e.g., L0-L4) that both human and orchestrator understand
2. How levels map to existing 14 gates (which gates fire at which level)
3. How levels are declared (at spawn time, inferred from skill/work-type, overridable)
4. How this REPLACES the current SkipConfig/flag-combo system
5. How tradeoff visibility (pressure points, architectural choices) fits WITHIN the levels framework
6. How --explain and --verify become judgment-driven rather than mechanical

WHAT TO AVOID:
- Adding MORE gates (we have 14, that's enough)
- Any design where the common case requires flags to pass completion
- Designs that remove orchestrator judgment in favor of automation
- Over-engineering — the simplest design that solves the vocabulary gap wins
- Treating tradeoff visibility as a separate system from verification levels

DELIVERABLE: Investigation with recommendations at .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-inv-architect-verification-levels.md

IN SCOPE: Design and recommendation
OUT OF SCOPE: Implementation

title: How does the orchestrator get architectural tradeoff visibility without reading code?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Dylan realized the 6-week registry cycle may have been partly driven by feature requests that implicitly demanded multi-source reconciliation — requests he couldn't push back on because he lacked visibility into the architectural tradeoffs agents were making. The question: how does the orchestrator surface and evaluate architectural tradeoffs (like 'cache vs query directly') without reading code? Models and probes are the current answer, but are they sufficient? What's missing?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Investigate how the orchestrator can get architectural tradeoff visibility without reading code. This is a strategic question (orch-go-1158) born from a concrete failure: the 6-week registry drift cycle (Dec 21 - Feb 18) happened partly because Dylan's feature requests implicitly demanded multi-source reconciliation, and no agent surfaced the tradeoff ('cache locally = fast but drifts' vs 'query directly = slow but correct'). Dylan doesn't read code by design (orchestrator constraint). Models and probes are the current mechanism for externalized architectural understanding, but the registry cycle proves they weren't sufficient — or weren't in place early enough. Investigate: (1) What tradeoff classes recur in this codebase? (2) When agents make architectural choices, what's the current surfacing mechanism? (3) What's missing — should agents be required to declare tradeoffs at spawn-time? Should models include 'pressure points' that flag when a feature request conflicts with architecture? (4) Concrete recommendation for how to close this gap. Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-18-two-lane-agent-discovery.md (the decision that finally resolved the drift), .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md (the model that should have caught this earlier). ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan can't push back on implementation approaches he's never told about. How does the system surface architectural tradeoffs to a non-code-reading orchestrator?

We forked steveyegge/beads and have local changes, but our documentation (beads-integration-architecture model, OSS relationship decision) says we dropped the fork and use upstream as-is. The docs are stale. We need to know what's actually true now and whether beads can be improved.

SESSION SCOPE: Medium

WHAT TO AUDIT:

2. BEADS INTEGRATION IN ORCH-GO:

   - How does orch-go shell out to bd vs use RPC?
   - What beads commands are called from orch-go code? (grep for 'bd ' and beads package usage)
   - How does the daemon interact with beads?

3. MODEL STALENESS:
   - Compare .kb/models/beads-integration-architecture.md claims against reality
   - Compare .kb/decisions/2025-12-21-beads-oss-relationship-clean-slate.md against reality (we clearly have a fork now)

4. IMPROVEMENT OPPORTUNITIES:
   - Read ~/.kb/principles.md — what principles apply to beads integration?
   - Read .kb/guides/beads-integration.md — what's working, what's friction?
   - Based on how we actually use beads, what features in the fork could be improved?
   - Are there beads capabilities we're not using that we should be?
   - Are there things orch-go works around that beads should handle natively?

FOR EACH FINDING:
- What exists (implementation)
- Whether it's documented accurately (model/guide staleness)
- Whether it could be improved (opportunity)

FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: Verify that GPT model spawning works end-to-end in tmux via opencode backend. We need to know what actually works before relying on these paths.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## What to Verify

Test each GPT model alias through the spawn pipeline:

1. **Model resolution** - Do codex, gpt-5, gpt-5.2 resolve to correct provider/model strings? Check pkg/model/model.go
2. **Spawn with tmux** - Does `orch spawn --bypass-triage --backend opencode --model codex --tmux hello 'say hello'` create a tmux window and connect?
3. **Spawn with tmux (gpt-5)** - Same test with `--model gpt-5`
4. **Headless spawn** - Does `orch spawn --bypass-triage --backend opencode --model codex hello 'say hello'` work without tmux?

## Test Protocol

For each combination:
- Run the spawn command
- Observe: Did it error? Did tmux window appear? Did the agent receive the prompt?
- If it fails, capture the exact error message
- Check if OpenCode server is running first (`curl -s http://localhost:4096/health` or similar)

## Scope Boundaries

IN: Testing spawn commands with GPT models, reading model resolution code, observing tmux behavior
OUT: Fixing any bugs found (just document them), testing Anthropic models, changing any code

## Deliverable

Probe in `.kb/models/` if a relevant model exists, otherwise investigation in `.kb/investigations/`. Document:
- Which combinations work
- Which combinations fail (with exact errors)
- Any surprising behavior

title: [orch-go] feature-impl: ORIENTATION_FRAME: GPT models can't be spawned ...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: GPT models can't be spawned in tmux because opencode attach doesn't support --model. This blocks visual monitoring of GPT agents.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## The Problem

BuildOpencodeAttachCommand() in pkg/tmux/tmux.go:276 passes --model to opencode attach, but that flag doesn't exist. Tmux spawn with non-default models times out after 15s.

## The Fix

Change the tmux+opencode spawn path to:
1. Pre-create the session via HTTP API (which DOES accept model) using the existing opencode client
2. Attach to the pre-created session using opencode attach --session <id>

This avoids needing to modify the OpenCode fork.

## Scope Boundaries

IN: Modifying pkg/tmux/tmux.go and cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go (or wherever tmux+opencode spawn is orchestrated) to pre-create session then attach
OUT: Changing OpenCode fork, modifying headless spawn path (that already works), changing model resolution

## Prior Evidence

From probe: .kb/models/model-access-spawn-paths/probes/2026-02-21-probe-gpt-model-spawn-e2e-verification.md
- opencode attach only supports: --dir, --continue, --session, --fork, --password
- BuildOpencodeAttachCommand() at pkg/tmux/tmux.go:276 incorrectly adds --model
- Headless spawn works because it uses HTTP API which accepts model

## Verification

After fix, this command should work:
orch spawn --bypass-triage --backend opencode --model codex --tmux hello 'say hello'

It should create a tmux window showing the opencode TUI with a codex session.

title: [orch-go] feature-impl: ORIENTATION_FRAME: The gpt-5 alias silently cre...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: The gpt-5 alias silently creates zombie sessions because the model isn't configured in OpenCode. Quick fix to prevent silent failures.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go
SESSION SCOPE: Small

## The Problem

pkg/model/model.go maps gpt-5 to some model string, but OpenCode's config (~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc) only has versioned variants: gpt-5.1, gpt-5.1-codex, gpt-5.1-codex-mini, gpt-5.1-codex-max, gpt-5.2, gpt-5.2-codex. Sessions created with the gpt-5 alias never process — 0 responses after 30s.

## The Fix

In pkg/model/model.go, remap the gpt-5 alias to gpt-5.2 (the latest versioned variant). If there's a gpt5 or gpt-5 entry, update it. Keep gpt-5.2 as an explicit alias too.

Also check: are there any other aliases that map to unconfigured models? Fix any you find.

## Scope Boundaries

IN: Changing model aliases in pkg/model/model.go, running model_test.go to verify
OUT: Changing OpenCode config, modifying spawn paths, anything beyond model resolution

## Verification

go test ./pkg/model/ -run TestResolve should pass with gpt-5 resolving to the same thing as gpt-5.2.

Also fixed gpt-5-mini → gpt-5.1-codex-mini, codex-latest → gpt-5.2-codex (both were pointing to unconfigured models). Added explicit gpt-5.1/gpt-5.2 aliases. title: Investigate: work-graph UI shows tmux-spawned agents as 'unassigned' — how does dashboard discover agent state across spawn modes?
description:
FRAME: The work-graph view shows tmux-spawned agents as 'unassigned', which makes the dashboard unreliable for understanding what's actually running. Since we're mostly spawning via tmux now, the dashboard needs to work with that reality.

QUESTIONS:
1. What data sources does the work-graph UI use to resolve agent assignment? (opencode sessions? beads? workspace files?)
2. When orch spawn --tmux creates an agent, what gets written that the UI could discover? (workspace metadata, beads issue update, registry?)
3. Where is the gap — does the UI only look at opencode sessions (missing tmux), or does the spawn path fail to write something the UI expects?
4. What's the right fix surface — should tmux spawns write more metadata, or should the UI query more sources?

SCOPE:
- IN: work-graph UI data flow, spawn_cmd.go tmux path, serve_agents.go data assembly
- OUT: Actually fixing it (that's a follow-up feature-impl)

DELIVERABLE: .kb/investigations/ artifact with data flow diagram and gap analysis

title: [orch-go] architect: Architect: Orchestrator skill behavioral compli...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Architect: Orchestrator skill behavioral compliance — agents load the skill, identify as orchestrators, but revert to Claude Code defaults at action time

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Orchestrator sessions correctly load the skill and can describe their role when asked, but at action time they use Task tool instead of orch spawn, and bd close instead of orch complete. The knowledge is present but doesn't stick under pressure. This undermines the entire orchestrator/worker separation.

EVIDENCE:
1. Toolshed orchestrator used Task tool (Explore subagent) when Dylan said 'spawn a worker' — should have used orch spawn
2. Same session used bd close directly instead of orch complete, bypassing all verification gates
3. When asked 'what is your role?', correctly answered 'I'm your orchestrator, I don't write code' — so the skill IS loaded

QUESTIONS:
1. Why do agents comply with identity declarations but not action constraints? Is this a salience problem (too much content, action rules buried), a competing-instruction problem (Claude Code system prompt encourages Task tool usage), or something else?
2. What structural changes to the skill would make action constraints stick? (e.g., moving critical rules to top, reducing total skill length, using different framing, action-space restriction patterns)
3. Should there be a minimal 'orchestrator actions' section that's extremely concise and placed for maximum salience, separate from the full strategic guidance?
4. Are there patterns from prompt engineering / constitutional AI / instruction hierarchy research that apply here?

SCOPE:
- IN: Orchestrator skill structure, Claude Code default behaviors that compete, prompt salience patterns, skill architecture
- OUT: Actually rewriting the skill (that's a follow-up). Changing Claude Code internals.

DELIVERABLE: .kb/investigations/ artifact with diagnosis and concrete structural recommendations for the skill rewrite

Recommended two-layer fix: (1) restructure skill with action-identity fusion at top, (2) add tool-layer enforcement via hooks. title: orch complete doesn't close the corresponding tmux window
description:
FRAME: After running orch complete on a tmux-spawned agent, the tmux window stays open. This leaves stale windows accumulating in the workers session, making it hard to tell what's actually running vs what's already been completed.

EXPECTED: orch complete closes the beads issue, archives the workspace, AND kills the tmux window.
ACTUAL: Issue closed, workspace archived, tmux window persists.
repro: 1. orch spawn --bypass-triage --tmux feature-impl 'task' --issue ID  2. Wait for agent to complete  3. orch complete <id>  4. tmux list-windows shows the window still open

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix: orch complete doesn't close the corresponding tmux window after completing an agent.

FRAME: After orch complete on a tmux-spawned agent, the tmux window persists. Stale windows accumulate in the workers session, making it hard to tell what's running vs completed.

REPRO: 1. orch spawn --bypass-triage --tmux feature-impl 'task' --issue ID  2. Wait for completion  3. orch complete <id>  4. tmux list-windows — window still there

EXPECTED: orch complete should kill the tmux window as part of cleanup.

LIKELY FIX SURFACE: The completion pipeline (complete_pipeline.go or complete_actions.go) needs a step that looks up the tmux window from workspace metadata and kills it. The workspace has .spawn_mode file indicating 'claude' (tmux) and the AGENT_MANIFEST.json likely has the window name. Use pkg/tmux/ to kill the window.

NOTE: The hotspot gate fired because complete_cmd.go is >1500 lines. The fix should NOT go in complete_cmd.go — target complete_actions.go or complete_pipeline.go instead.

SCOPE:
- IN: complete_actions.go, complete_pipeline.go, pkg/tmux/ — add tmux window cleanup
- OUT: Refactoring complete_cmd.go, dashboard changes

title: Add tmux liveness check to query engine for claude-backend agents
description:
FRAME: Claude-backend agents show as 'dead' on the dashboard because the query engine only checks OpenCode sessions for liveness. Fix: when spawn_mode=claude and session_id is empty, check tmux window existence instead.

Root cause documented in orch-go-1177 probe: .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model/probes/2026-02-24-probe-claude-spawn-dashboard-visibility-gap.md

Fix surface: query_tracked.go joinWithReasonCodes() line 296-302, pkg/tmux/ for window existence check.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Add tmux liveness check to query engine for claude-backend agents

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Every tmux/claude-backend agent spawned since Feb 21 shows as 'dead' on the dashboard because the query engine only checks OpenCode sessions for liveness. Claude-backend agents don't have OpenCode sessions — their liveness signal is the tmux window.

ROOT CAUSE (from orch-go-1177 probe):
- runSpawnClaude never calls AtomicSpawnPhase2, so manifest has no session_id
- queryTrackedAgents in query_tracked.go marks missing session_id as 'missing_session' → dead
- SpawnMode field exists in manifest but query engine never uses it

FIX:
In joinWithReasonCodes() (query_tracked.go ~line 296-302), when manifest.SessionID == '' AND manifest.SpawnMode == 'claude':
- Check if tmux window exists matching the workspace name (use pkg/tmux/)
- If window exists → status = 'active'
- If window doesn't exist → status = 'dead'

The workspace name already maps to the tmux window name. No new manifest fields needed.

FILES:
- cmd/orch/query_tracked.go — joinWithReasonCodes() around line 296-302
- pkg/tmux/tmux.go — may need a WindowExists() helper

SCOPE:
- IN: query_tracked.go tmux liveness fallback, pkg/tmux helper if needed
- OUT: Changing spawn paths, manifest schema changes, dashboard UI changes

VERIFICATION: After fix, confirm orch status shows tmux-spawned agents as active (not dead). Run go build and go vet.

Window alive → status=active, window missing → status=dead. 3 new tests + 16 existing tests pass. go build and go vet clean. Tests: go test ./cmd/orch/ -run TestJoinWithReasonCodes - 16 passed, 0 failed (0.015s). No discovered work.

title: 1182 regression: dashboard oscillates between correct agent status and 'unassigned' on polling cycle
description:
FRAME: The tmux liveness check from orch-go-1182 is causing the dashboard to oscillate between correct and incorrect agent status on every polling cycle. This is worse than the original bug (consistently showing dead) because the UI is now unstable.

LIKELY CAUSE: The tmux liveness check (FindWindowByWorkspaceNameAllSessions) is intermittently failing or timing out, causing alternating responses from the /api/agents endpoint. When the check succeeds → correct status. When it fails/times out → falls back to 'unassigned'.

BLOCKS: orch-go-1182 should not be considered complete until this is fixed.
repro: 1. Open /work-graph view  2. Watch without touching anything  3. Agent cards cycle between 'Awaiting review (Phase: Complete)' with Verify badges and 'unassigned' with no badges  4. Cycle repeats on dashboard polling interval

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix regression: dashboard oscillates between correct agent status and 'unassigned' on every polling cycle

FRAME: The tmux liveness check added in orch-go-1182 (commit 4cb0158bd) is causing the /api/agents endpoint to return different results on alternating requests. The work-graph UI cycles between showing agents with 'Awaiting review (Phase: Complete)' + Verify badges, and showing them as 'unassigned' with no badges. No user interaction — pure polling cycle oscillation.

REGRESSION FROM: orch-go-1182 — added tmux liveness check in joinWithReasonCodes() for claude-backend agents

LIKELY CAUSE: FindWindowByWorkspaceNameAllSessions is intermittently failing, timing out, or returning inconsistent results. When the check succeeds → correct status with Phase: Complete. When it fails → no liveness data → 'unassigned'.

DEBUG APPROACH:
1. Check what FindWindowByWorkspaceNameAllSessions actually does — is it shelling out to tmux on every API request? That could be slow/flaky.
2. Hit /api/agents twice rapidly and diff the responses
3. Check if the tmux command has race conditions or timeout issues
4. Consider caching the tmux liveness result for a few seconds instead of checking on every request

FILES:

title: [orch-go] architect: Architect: Dashboard oscillation persists after...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Architect: Dashboard oscillation persists after two fix attempts — is the tmux liveness approach fundamentally wrong?

ORIENTATION_FRAME: We added tmux liveness checking to the dashboard query engine (orch-go-1182) so claude-backend agents would show up correctly. It caused oscillation. A second fix (orch-go-1183) added a 10s TTL cache. The oscillation persists. We've now got a cache in a codebase that spent 6 weeks fighting caches (see two-lane decision below). Something is structurally wrong.

HISTORY:
1. orch-go-1177 investigation: runSpawnClaude skips AtomicSpawnPhase2, so no session_id → query engine marks as dead
2. orch-go-1182: Added tmux liveness check in joinWithReasonCodes when spawn_mode=claude
3. orch-go-1183: Added 10s TTL cache + unified buildActiveAgentMap to use globalTrackedAgentsCache
4. Dashboard STILL oscillates between correct status and 'unassigned'

CRITICAL CONTEXT — Two-Lane Decision (.kb/decisions/2026-02-18-two-lane-agent-discovery.md):
- 'No other persisted lifecycle state allowed'
- In-memory cache allowed at 1-5s TTL only
- 'Tmux owns: Presentation layer. Does NOT own: Any state whatsoever'
- The decision explicitly says tmux is UI only, not state
- 6 weeks of cache/remove cycles led to this decision

QUESTIONS:
1. Does checking tmux for liveness violate the two-lane decision? The decision says 'Tmux owns presentation layer, does NOT own any state whatsoever.' We're now using tmux as a liveness signal.
2. Should runSpawnClaude call AtomicSpawnPhase2 instead? The original root cause is that the claude spawn path skips the session_id write. Fixing the spawn path means the existing OpenCode liveness check would work.
3. Is there a simpler approach that doesn't require tmux liveness at all? E.g., writing a session_id to the manifest even for claude-backend agents, or using a different liveness signal.
4. Should we revert 1182 and 1183 entirely and fix the spawn path instead?

SCOPE:
- IN: Two-lane decision compliance, spawn path analysis, alternative approaches to claude-backend visibility
- OUT: Actually implementing the fix

DELIVERABLE: .kb/investigations/ artifact with architectural recommendation — fix spawn path vs fix query path vs something else entirely

title: Enforce investigation → architect → implementation sequence for hotspot areas
description:
FRAME: We just reverted two implementations (orch-go-1182/1183) because a worker jumped from investigation findings directly to code, violating the two-lane architectural decision. The hotspot gate fired and was overridden with --force-hotspot. This is a systemic gap — investigations discover what's wrong, but only architects can decide how to fix it within existing constraints.

EVIDENCE:
- orch-go-1182: Worker implemented tmux liveness check directly from 1177 investigation
- orch-go-1183: Second worker tried to fix 1182's regression with caching
- orch-go-1184: Architect found both violated two-lane decision, recommended completely different approach
- The hotspot gate fired on 1182 and recommended architect first — we overrode it

DESIRED OUTCOME: When an investigation produces findings that recommend code changes in hotspot areas, the system should enforce architect review before implementation. This could be:
- Structural: hotspot gate blocks --force-hotspot when spawning from investigation issues
- Workflow: investigation completion creates architect issue automatically when in hotspot area
- Skill-level: investigation skill recommends architect follow-up instead of implementation follow-up for hotspot areas
- Orchestrator-level: orchestrator skill constraint about the sequence

Constraint recorded: kb-8d997c

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Architect: Design enforcement for investigation → architect → implementation sequence in hotspot areas

ORIENTATION_FRAME: We just reverted two implementations (orch-go-1182/1183) because a worker jumped from investigation findings directly to code, violating the two-lane architectural decision. The hotspot gate fired and was overridden. This cost us 3 spawn cycles and a regression. The gap: investigations discover what's wrong, but only architects can decide how to fix it within existing constraints. We need this sequence enforced, not just documented.

EVIDENCE:
- orch-go-1177: Investigation correctly found root cause (runSpawnClaude skips AtomicSpawnPhase2)
- orch-go-1182: Worker implemented tmux liveness check directly from investigation — violated two-lane decision
- orch-go-1183: Second worker added cache to fix regression — deepened violation
- orch-go-1184: Architect caught both violations, recommended completely different approach (phase-based liveness)
- Hotspot gate fired on 1182, was overridden with --force-hotspot

DESIGN SPACE:
1. Structural enforcement in orch spawn: when spawning feature-impl/systematic-debugging from an investigation issue in a hotspot area, require architect first (block --force-hotspot, or require --force-hotspot + architect issue reference)
2. Investigation skill output: investigations in hotspot areas should recommend architect follow-up, not implementation follow-up
3. Orchestrator skill constraint: the orchestrator should route investigation→architect→implementation, never investigation→implementation for hotspots
4. Completion gate: orch complete for investigations could create architect issues automatically when hotspot files are in scope

Consider the tradeoff between enforcement strictness and spawn friction. Not every investigation needs an architect — only those in hotspot areas or those whose recommendations touch architectural decisions.

SCOPE:
- IN: Enforcement mechanism design, spawn gate modifications, skill/workflow changes
- OUT: Actually implementing the chosen mechanism

DELIVERABLE: .kb/investigations/ artifact with recommended enforcement approach and implementation plan

title: Architect: Should feature-impl skill use Claude Code plan mode for implementation planning?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Agent 1169 (architect skill, daemon-spawned) spontaneously entered Claude Code plan mode — wrote a plan, presented it for approval, then executed after approval. This is interesting because our feature-impl skill has its own planning phase but doesn't leverage Claude Code's native plan mode. The question: should feature-impl integrate plan mode as its planning mechanism?

OBSERVATIONS:
- 1169 used plan mode naturally (architect skill, not feature-impl) — Claude Code's native affordance
- Feature-impl currently has a Planning phase that's prompt-driven, not tool-driven
- Plan mode gives the user a visible approval gate before implementation starts
- This could replace or complement the existing phase-based planning in feature-impl

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. Does plan mode's approval gate align with our phase model (Planning → Implementation)?
2. Would this improve or complicate the daemon-driven workflow (daemon can't approve plans)?
3. Is this better than the current approach where agents self-report phase transitions?
4. What's the interaction between plan mode and our skill's phase reporting protocol?

CONSTRAINT: Daemon-spawned agents run headless — plan mode approval requires human interaction. This may only work for --tmux or --inline spawns.

Agent 1169 (architect skill) spontaneously used plan mode — wrote a plan, got human approval, then executed. Feature-impl currently has prompt-driven Planning phase. Investigate: (1) alignment with phase model, (2) daemon compatibility (headless agents can't get plan approval), (3) whether this improves quality vs current self-reported phases, (4) interaction with skill phase reporting protocol. See .kb/investigations/ for feature-impl skill design. Produce recommendation with trade-offs.

comments.text: Phase: Complete - Produced design investigation recommending AGAINST integrating Claude Code plan mode into feature-impl. Four evaluation dimensions analyzed: (1) fundamental misalignment with phase model (prompt-driven vs tool-enforced, workspace artifacts vs ephemeral plan files), (2) daemon incompatibility (plan mode requires interactive approval, daemon spawns are headless), (3) marginal quality improvement not worth operational costs (feature-impl's Investigation+Design phases already better for orchestrated agents), (4) breaks phase reporting (bash blocked → no bd comments → agent appears unresponsive). Alternative recommendation: strengthen prompt-level planning enforcement in feature-impl skill. Probe extends agent-lifecycle-state-model with new incompatibility class. No discovered work.

comments.text: CONSTRAINT: Accounts have asymmetric capacity. Work=20x Max, Personal=5x Max. Naive 'most headroom' algorithm is wrong — 50% of 20x is way more absolute capacity than 50% of 5x. Personal's 5-hour limit will bind fast under load. Strategy must be tier-aware: work=primary (absorbs bulk), personal=spillover (used when work is rate-limited). accounts.yaml needs tier and role fields. Percentages alone are insufficient for routing decisions.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Design automatic account distribution for Claude CLI spawns. Two accounts: work (20x Max, primary) and personal (5x Max, spillover). Current state: manual claude-personal alias switches CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR. Need: daemon and orch spawn auto-select account based on tier-aware capacity routing. Work absorbs bulk, personal is spillover when work is rate-limited. Design must cover: (1) accounts.yaml schema changes (tier, role fields), (2) spawn-time account selection algorithm, (3) env var injection for Claude CLI subprocess (CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR), (4) interactive session guidance (which claude to launch). See issue comments for full constraints.

title: orch complete kills wrong tmux window when multiple agents share session
description: Hypothesis: orch complete kills tmux window by window number (renumberable) instead of window ID (@-prefixed, stable). When an earlier window is killed, tmux renumbers remaining windows, and subsequent complete/interact operations target wrong window. Alternate hypothesis: Claude CLI spawn never actually started (same class as 1215 AtomicSpawnPhase2 bug). Check: does orch complete use window name or @ID for tmux kill? Does spawn record @ID or window number?
repro: 1. Spawn two agents: 1215 (window 2, @697) and 1214 (window 4, @700) in workers-orch-go. 2. Complete 1215 via orch complete — closes window 2. 3. tmux renumbers: window 4 becomes window 3. 4. 1214 architect now has no tmux window — process is dead, status shows idle/at-risk.

title: orch clean --sessions kills active daemon-spawned tmux windows
description: orch clean --sessions treats daemon-spawned Claude CLI tmux windows as stale because it checks for active OpenCode sessions (0 found) and assumes any remaining tmux windows are orphaned. Daemon spawns via Claude CLI in tmux, not OpenCode API, so its windows have no corresponding OpenCode session. Fix: cross-reference tmux windows against in_progress beads issues before killing.
repro: 1. Run orch daemon run (spawns agents via Claude CLI in tmux). 2. Run orch clean --sessions. 3. Observe it kills the daemon's active agent windows (e.g. 1217, 1218) reporting them as stale.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Fix orch clean --sessions killing active daemon-spawned tmux windows. Bug: clean --sessions checks for OpenCode sessions (finds 0 for Claude CLI spawns) and assumes remaining tmux windows are orphaned. Fix: before killing a tmux window, check if its beads ID has an in_progress issue — if so, skip it. Repro: 1) daemon spawns via Claude CLI in tmux, 2) run orch clean --sessions, 3) observe active agent windows killed. Relevant code: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go.

title: ListOpenIssues() has hidden 50-issue limit - FallbackList does not pass --limit 0
description: pkg/verify/beads_api.go ListOpenIssues() calls beads.FallbackList('') which runs bd list --json without --limit flag. Default limit is 50. Projects with many issues miss open ones. Fix: pass --limit 0 to FallbackList or use CLIClient which already handles this.
repro: Call verify.ListOpenIssues() in a project with >50 issues. Only 50 are returned. Open/in_progress issues beyond top 50 are missing.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Bug: pkg/verify/beads_api.go ListOpenIssues() calls beads.FallbackList('') which runs 'bd list --json' without --limit flag. Default limit is 50 issues. Projects with >50 issues silently truncate results. Fix: pass --limit 0 to FallbackList or use CLIClient which already handles this. Verify by checking that all open issues are returned in projects with many issues.

title: Daemon needs single-instance guard (PID file or lock)
description: Multiple daemon processes accumulate silently (found 3 running concurrently from different sessions). They fight over the status file and spawns, causing the daemon to appear running but not actually pick up work. Fix: use a PID file or flock-based lock so only one daemon instance runs at a time. Second invocation should either fail fast or kill the old one.
repro: Start orch daemon run in multiple terminals or sessions. ps aux shows multiple orch daemon run processes. None reliably picks up triage:ready issues.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Daemon needs single-instance guard (PID file or lock)

Multiple daemon processes accumulate silently (found 3 running concurrently from different sessions). They fight over the status file and spawns, causing the daemon to appear running but not actually pick up work. Fix: use a PID file or flock-based lock so only one daemon instance runs at a time. Second invocation should either fail fast or kill the old one.

comments.text:
FRAME: Implement the fix for cross-project 'unassigned' in work-graph, designed by architect in orch-go-1228.

ROOT CAUSE (from architect analysis):
buildActiveAgentMap() in cmd/orch/serve_beads.go:1047 has two scoping gaps:
1. listTrackedIssues() only queries LOCAL beads — cross-project issues (toolshed-*, etc.) are missing
2. client.ListSessions('') only returns default-project sessions — agents spawned for other projects are invisible

FIX (architect-specified):

2. Replace client.ListSessions('') with the existing listSessionsAcrossProjects() function which already handles multi-project session aggregation
3. This ensures cross-project issues get matched to their active agents

title: Orchestrator skill not loaded when launching cc personal in non-orch-go project
description: When launching Claude Code via 'cc personal --allowed-tools * --dangerously-skip-permissions' in the toolshed project directory, the orchestrator SKILL.md at ~/.claude/skills/meta/orchestrator/SKILL.md is not injected into the session context. Claude responds as a generic collaborator instead of assuming the orchestrator role. The cc function correctly sets CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator, but the skill content never reaches the model. Need to investigate: (1) how Claude Code discovers/loads skills from ~/.claude/skills/, (2) whether CLAUDE_CONTEXT env var is supposed to trigger skill loading or if a separate mechanism is needed, (3) whether this is a Claude Code limitation or a missing hook/config.
repro: 1. cd ~/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/toolshed  2. cc personal --allowed-tools '*' --dangerously-skip-permissions  3. Ask 'what is your role?'  4. Claude responds as generic collaborator, not orchestrator

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Investigate why the orchestrator skill (at ~/.claude/skills/meta/orchestrator/SKILL.md) is not loaded when launching Claude Code via 'cc personal' in non-orch-go projects. Map the current skill injection mechanism end-to-end: the cc() function in ~/.zshrc sets CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator, but how does the SKILL.md content actually reach the model? Check: (1) ~/.orch/hooks/ for any session-start hooks that inject skills, (2) ~/.claude/hooks/ for Claude Code hooks, (3) any python scripts that load on session start, (4) whether Claude Code has native skill discovery from ~/.claude/skills/. Then evaluate two approaches: (A) Keep the current hook/script mechanism but fix it for cross-project use, vs (B) Have the cc() wrapper in ~/.zshrc directly inject the skill content (e.g., via --system-prompt or appending to a temp CLAUDE.md). Recommend the simpler, more reliable approach. Reference issue: orch-go-1233.

title: Fix orchestrator skill not loading in non-orch-go projects (double-gate failure)
description: Two bugs in load-orchestration-context.py prevent orchestrator skill from loading in non-orch-go projects. Bug 1: is_spawned_agent() checks CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator to detect spawned agents, but cc personal also sets that for interactive sessions — hook exits early. Bug 2: skill loading gated behind find_orch_directory() requiring .orch/ dir, but orchestrator skill is project-independent. Fix: (1) add ORCH_SPAWNED=1 env var in claude.go:87, (2) check ORCH_SPAWNED instead of CLAUDE_CONTEXT in hook line 496, (3) restructure main() to load skill before .orch/ gate. title: Architect: design project group model for orch ecosystem (parent workdirs, sibling kb context, cross-project spawn scope)
description:
Orch-go has 19 registered projects as a flat list with no grouping concept. Three converging needs require a project group model:

1. **Sibling kb context in spawn** — spawning into toolshed should surface price-watch kb artifacts automatically when the task is relevant. Currently only searches target project's .kb/. Need to know which projects are 'siblings' to search.

2. **Parent workdir for cross-repo orchestration** — decision (2026-02-25-cross-repo-orchestration-from-parent.md) says scs-special-projects is the coordination point for work projects. But orch-go doesn't know that toolshed/price-watch/specs-platform/sendassist are children of scs-special-projects.

3. **Spawn/daemon scoping** — the daemon polls ALL registered projects. When orchestrating from scs-special-projects, should it only see work projects? Account routing (personal vs work) also maps to project groups.

Current implicit groups (by path):
- Orch ecosystem (personal): orch-go, opencode, beads, kb-cli, kn, orch-cli, etc. Home: orch-go
- SCS work: toolshed, price-watch, specs-platform, sendassist, scs-slack. Parent: scs-special-projects  
- Other personal: blog, snap, dotfiles

Questions to answer:
- Should groups be explicit config or inferred from directory structure?
- Does the parent dir (scs-special-projects) need a special role, or is 'group' enough?
- How do groups affect: kb context search scope, daemon polling, dashboard filtering, spawn account selection?
- What's the minimal viable model that unblocks cross-project kb context without over-engineering?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: The spawn pipeline's kb context matching (used to populate PRIOR KNOWLEDGE in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md) only searches the target project's .kb/ directory. When spawning into toolshed with a task about 'pricing simulation', it won't find price-watch's competitive-simulation-api investigation even though it's highly relevant. Design a solution for cross-project kb context in spawn. Investigate: (1) How does kb context matching currently work? Find the code in orch-go that generates the PRIOR KNOWLEDGE section — what does it query, how does it search? (2) What are sibling projects? Projects under the same parent dir? All registered kb projects? Should this be configurable? (3) What's the right scope — searching ALL 19 registered projects is too broad, but searching only the target is too narrow. (4) Performance — kb context search is already in the spawn hot path, adding N more project searches could slow it down. (5) Relevance filtering — cross-project hits should be clearly labeled as coming from another project. Reference issue: orch-go-1235. Decision context: .kb/decisions/ and /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/.kb/decisions/2026-02-25-cross-repo-orchestration-from-parent.md

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Investigating kb context matching in spawn pipeline for cross-project design

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design the project group model for orch-go. Three needs converge: (1) sibling kb context — spawning into toolshed should auto-surface price-watch artifacts when relevant, (2) parent workdir — scs-special-projects is the coordination point for work projects but orch-go doesn't know about parent-child relationships, (3) spawn/daemon scoping — daemon polls all 19 projects flat, no way to scope by group or route accounts.

Current implicit groups by path:
- Orch ecosystem (personal): orch-go, opencode, beads, kb-cli, kn, etc.
- SCS work: toolshed, price-watch, specs-platform, sendassist, scs-slack. Parent: scs-special-projects
- Other personal: blog, snap, dotfiles

Investigate:
1. How kb context matching works today (find the code in orch-go or kb-cli that generates PRIOR KNOWLEDGE)
2. How the daemon discovers projects (ProjectRegistry in project_resolution.go)
3. How spawn determines which project to search for context
4. What minimal config would express project groups — could be in kb, in .orch/config.yaml, or inferred from directory structure
5. How groups cascade to: kb context search scope, daemon polling, dashboard filtering, account selection

Design the simplest model that solves all three needs. Consider: explicit config vs convention (parent dir inference). Reference: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/.kb/decisions/2026-02-25-cross-repo-orchestration-from-parent.md and the cross-repo consequences probe at .kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/probes/2026-02-25-probe-cross-repo-orchestration-consequences.md

title: Architect: reconcile Claude Code auto-memory with kb-cli knowledge externalization
description: Claude Code has a new 'auto memory' feature (MEMORY.md at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/) where the agent writes self-notes that persist across sessions. We already have kb-cli for knowledge externalization (decisions, constraints, tried/failed, investigations, guides, probes). These overlap — both store learned patterns and preferences that persist across sessions. An architect should decide: (1) Should auto-memory be disabled entirely in favor of kb-cli? (2) Should each have a distinct lane (e.g., auto-memory for session-ephemeral context recovery, kb-cli for durable cross-agent knowledge)? (3) Does auto-memory's 200-line startup injection compete with or complement CLAUDE.md instructions? (4) Risk: auto-memory is per-user and invisible to spawned agents — kb-cli artifacts are discoverable by all agents via kb context. Is that a feature or a bug? Reference: the Knowledge Placement table in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md already defines where different knowledge types go, but doesn't account for auto-memory.

title: Fix orchestrator skill: Gate 2 behavioral verification conflicts with frame guard code-read block
description: The orchestrator skill says Gate 2 for features is 'Run it, see it work' and for bugs is 'Reproduce original bug, confirm fixed.' But the frame guard (gate-orchestrator-code-access.py) blocks reading .go/.ts/etc files. This creates a loop: agent tries to spot-check code → gets blocked → falls back to trusting orch complete automated checks → which is actually fine, but the skill doesn't say that. Fix: update the Gate 2 section to clarify that behavioral verification for orchestrators means (1) orch complete --verified automated gates, (2) SYNTHESIS.md review, (3) running user-facing features if applicable, (4) spawning a verification investigation if automated gates are insufficient. NOT reading source code. Related: orch-go-1238 (explanation style update to same skill).
repro: Orchestrator runs orch complete, then tries to spot-check code for behavioral gate. Frame guard blocks the Read tool on .ts/.go files. Agent falls back to trusting automated checks, which is correct but undocumented.

title: Architect: design code review gate for agent completion pipeline
description:
Current completion review is a knowledge management ceremony (reconnection, model updates, probe verdicts) not a code quality gate. The only code quality checks are: tests pass, go build succeeds, accretion check. Nobody reads the diff. SYNTHESIS.md is the agent's self-report — like approving a PR by reading the description and seeing CI green.

Questions for architect:
1. Should we introduce a code-review skill/agent as part of the completion pipeline?
2. When should it trigger? Every completion? Only full-tier? Only hotspot areas? Only features/bugs (not investigations)?
3. What should it check? Architectural violations, security, edge cases, coupling, correctness vs intent?
4. How should the orchestrator consume review findings? Block completion? Advisory? Separate beads issue for fixes?
5. Cost/benefit: doubled token spend per completion vs catching bugs that spawn 3 more agents to fix later.
6. Should this replace or augment the current Gate 2 behavioral verification?

Related: orch-go-1246 (Gate 2 conflicts with frame guard). The current system has the orchestrator trying to spot-check code but being blocked by the frame guard. A dedicated code-review agent would resolve this tension — the orchestrator stays strategic, a worker does the actual review.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design a code review gate for the agent completion pipeline.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: The orchestrator's completion review is a knowledge management ceremony, not a code quality gate. Nobody reads the diff — the only checks are tests pass, build succeeds, accretion check. SYNTHESIS.md is the agent's self-report. This is like approving a PR by reading the description and seeing CI green. Meanwhile the orchestrator frame guard blocks reading code, creating a tension where Gate 2 (behavioral verification) can't actually verify behavior.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

SESSION SCOPE:
Design whether and how to introduce code review into the agent completion pipeline.

IN SCOPE:
- Analyze current completion pipeline (orch complete flow, verification gates, Gate 2)
- Evaluate: should a code-review agent/skill exist as part of completion?
- When should it trigger (every completion? full-tier only? hotspot areas? features/bugs only?)
- What should it check (architectural violations, security, coupling, correctness vs intent, edge cases?)
- How should orchestrator consume findings (block completion? advisory? spawn fix issues?)
- Cost/benefit analysis: token spend vs catching downstream bugs
- Relationship to existing Gate 2 behavioral verification and frame guard
- Relationship to orch-go-1246 (Gate 2 conflicts with frame guard)

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Implementing the code review system
- Modifying the orchestrator skill (that's orch-go-1246)
- CI/CD integration (this is about the agent pipeline, not deployment)

DELIVERABLES:
- Decision record at .kb/decisions/ with recommendation, rejected alternatives, and consequences
- Clear specification of when code review triggers and what it checks

PRIOR ARTIFACTS:
- Orchestrator skill: ~/.claude/skills/SKILL.md (section 5: At Completion, Gate 2)
- Frame guard: ~/.orch/hooks/gate-orchestrator-code-access.py

title: Consolidate quick entry clusters into skill guidance

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: kb reflect identified 20 clusters of quick entries (agent:25, session:18, spawn:18, beads:16, spawn-context:15, skill:15) that represent knowledge trapped in JSONL rather than in the skill guidance agents actually receive.

These are practical constraints and decisions that workers encounter repeatedly. Consolidating them into skill templates means agents get the guidance at spawn time instead of hoping kb context surfaces the right entry.

Approach: For each cluster, review entries, identify load-bearing guidance, and add to relevant skill template. Supersede entries that are absorbed. This is an instance of the continuous maintenance pattern (decision: .kb/decisions/2026-02-25-continuous-knowledge-maintenance.md).

Top priority clusters: agent (25), session (18), spawn (18) — these represent the most repeated friction.

title: Tmux follower mechanism is broken
description: The tmux follower mechanism is broken. Needs investigation to determine root cause and fix.
repro: Dylan reports tmux follower mechanism is currently broken

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Fix: Ghostty workers window doesn't follow orchestrator window switches.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan has a two-Ghostty-window setup — orchestrator on the left, workers on the right. When he switches orchestrator tmux windows (e.g., from orch-go to toolshed), the workers Ghostty should auto-switch to workers-toolshed. This is broken.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

SESSION SCOPE: Debug and fix the tmux follower mechanism.

DIAGNOSTICS ALREADY DONE:
- Dashboard API (/api/context) works correctly — returns right project
- Hook is registered: after-select-window[0] run-shell -b ~/.local/bin/sync-workers-session.sh
- CWD detection works (pane_current_path returns correct path)
- Manual tmux switch-client -c /dev/ttys001 -t workers-kb-cli WORKS
- Script works when run manually via bash -x
- Workers sessions exist: workers-orch-go, workers-kb-cli, workers-price-watch, workers-toolshed
- Two clients attached: /dev/ttys000=orchestrator, /dev/ttys001=workers-orch-go

HYPOTHESIS: The hook fires via run-shell -b which runs in a background context. Something about that context (different tmux session/client context, environment, or timing) prevents the switch-client from working. The script may be detecting the wrong session_name or wrong client TTY when run from the hook vs manually.

KEY FILES:
- ~/.local/bin/sync-workers-session.sh — the sync script
- ~/.tmux.conf.local — hook configuration
- pkg/tmux/follower.go — Go implementation of CWD detection
- .kb/models/follow-orchestrator-mechanism/model.md — full architecture doc

DELIVERABLES:
- Working Ghostty follow: switching orchestrator windows causes workers Ghostty to follow
- Root cause documented

VERIFICATION: Switch orchestrator windows between projects and confirm workers Ghostty follows.

comments.text: Phase: Planning - Reading sync script and tmux hook config to understand current mechanism

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Root cause confirmed: two bugs. (1) run-shell -b background context causes tmux display-message to resolve wrong session name (racy client resolution). (2) Directory basename 'scs-special-projects' doesn't match workers session name 'workers-toolshed'. Fix: pass context via hook format expansion + add config-based session name resolution.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Fixed two bugs in Ghostty workers follow mechanism. (1) run-shell -b loses tmux client context: display-message inside background scripts resolves to arbitrary client. Fix: pass context via hook format expansion arguments. (2) Directory basename 'scs-special-projects' \!= workers session name 'workers-toolshed'. Fix: 3-tier session name resolution (config → basename → reverse lookup). Tests: manual simulation verified both orch-go↔toolshed switch directions. Full end-to-end requires Dylan to switch windows from orchestrator Ghostty to verify. No discovered work.
