title: Architect: skillc deploy path keeps silently failing — agents run with stale skills

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Architect: skillc deploy path keeps silently failing — agents run with stale skills

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Agents have been running with week-old skill content because skillc deploy silently puts files in the wrong path. This has happened multiple times. The blast radius is invisible — no error, no detection, agents just get stale guidance and we don't know until we spot-check during completion review.

Known instances:
- This session: 1244 and 1252 both claimed 'deployed via skillc' but deployed SKILL.md files were from Feb 18 (a week stale). Pruning guidance, consolidated entries — none reached agents.
- The deploy path issue: skillc deploy --target ~/.claude/skills/ now nests the full source structure (skills/src/worker/...) instead of flattening to {category}/{skill}/. Requires per-category invocation.

Questions for architect:
1. Why does skillc deploy keep changing path behavior? Is it a regression or intended?
2. What verification gate catches this? (e.g., post-deploy checksum comparison, timestamp assertion)
3. Should deploy be atomic — succeed fully or fail loudly?
4. Should orch spawn verify skill freshness at spawn time? (compare deployed vs source checksums)
5. Is the right fix in skillc itself, in the deploy workflow, or in a verification layer?

Cross-repo: skillc source is in ~/orch-knowledge (Go binary). Deployed skills read from ~/.claude/skills/{category}/{skill}/SKILL.md. orch-go loads via pkg/skills/loader.go.

SKILL: architect — this is a design problem, not a one-line fix.

title: Daemon spawn: use issue title as TASK, not FRAME comment
description: Daemon constructs spawn prompts by putting the FRAME beads comment as the TASK field. Since FRAME comments start with 'FRAME:' or 'ORIENTATION_FRAME:', the workspace name slug becomes 'orientation-frame-...' for every spawn. Fix: use the beads issue TITLE as the TASK field. Put the FRAME comment as a separate ORIENTATION_FRAME: section after TASK. This matches how manual orch spawn works (TASK: is the title, ORIENTATION_FRAME: is separate context). Files: pkg/daemon/daemon.go or wherever daemon constructs the spawn prompt.
repro: Daemon spawns agents where TASK field starts with ORIENTATION_FRAME: or FRAME: from beads comment. Workspace name slugifier picks this up, making all tmux windows start with 'orientation-frame...' which are indistinguishable. Example: og-arch-orientation-frame-dylan-20feb-9c0f, scsppr-inv-orientation-frame-ve-26feb-557f.

title: Activate Defect-Class metadata in kb reflect / synthesis pipeline

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Agents are already tagging investigations with Defect-Class (configuration-drift, integration-mismatch, unbounded-growth) — inspired by the Defect Class Blindness principle — but nothing reads it. The whole point of the principle is cross-investigation pattern detection. This issue makes that metadata active so kb reflect or synthesis can group investigations by defect class and surface recurring root causes.

CURRENT STATE:
- 7 investigations have Defect-Class field (added organically by agents, not from template)
- Values seen: configuration-drift (5), integration-mismatch (1), unbounded-growth (1)
- No Go code, skill template, or kb reflect logic consumes the field
- Not in the investigation template — agents add it on their own

WHAT ACTIVE MEANS:
- kb reflect --type synthesis should be able to group investigations by defect class
- Surface when multiple investigations share a defect class (the exact failure mode the principle warns about)
- Consider: standardize the field in the investigation template so it's consistently present
- Consider: taxonomy of defect classes vs free-form tagging

title: Add slave DB client to toolshed + evaluate dataclip replacement
description: Two goals: (1) Add read-only slave/follower DB client to toolshed's pkg/scsdb. Currently only DATABASE_URL exists (single connection). Need a SLAVE_DATABASE_URL or DATABASE_FOLLOWER_URL for read-only queries against the Heroku follower. (2) Evaluate replacing Heroku dataclip with custom query via the slave DB — more flexibility and establishes a reusable pattern for direct DB queries. Existing code: pkg/scsdb/ has client.go, config.go, types.go, client_test.go. Design questions: connection pooling strategy for read replica, whether to expose as separate client or add ReadOnly() method to existing client, how to handle failover, env var naming convention. This should be a reusable pattern across SCS projects.

title: Hotspot gate: accept prior architect review as bypass evidence
description: Hotspot gate blocks feature-impl on files >1500 lines, requiring architect review first. But when an architect HAS already reviewed (completed workspace exists, closed issue exists), the gate can't recognize it. The gate needs a --architect-ref flag or automatic detection of prior architect completion for the same area.
repro: Agent in beads project had architect review completed (workspace be-arch-investigate-recommend-id-26feb-9695), tried to spawn feature-impl on memory.go (1669 lines). Gate blocked it. Agent thrashed 5+ attempts: changed issue type, created fake architect issue, fought bd close hook. Burned most of session budget on gate-fighting.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Gates that can't accept valid evidence force agents into workaround loops. The hotspot gate is correct in principle (bloated files need architect review before tactical work) but broken in practice (no way to say 'architect already reviewed this'). This is an instance of the configuration-drift defect class — the gate's model of 'architect review happened' doesn't match how architect reviews actually complete in the system.

DESIGN DIRECTION:
1. --architect-ref <workspace-or-issue> flag on orch spawn that the gate accepts
2. OR: automatic detection — check for closed architect workspaces targeting the same file/area
3. The gate should log WHY it blocked (what evidence it looked for and didn't find) so agents can satisfy it directly instead of guessing

title: Implement kb create probe command in kb-cli
orch clean kills active Claude Code tmux agents when OpenCode has no sessions
description: orch clean's stale tmux window detection assumes tmux windows map to OpenCode sessions. But claude-mode agents run Claude Code CLI directly in tmux — they have no OpenCode session. When OpenCode server restarts (0 sessions) or for claude-mode agents, clean incorrectly classifies active windows as stale. The fix: clean should check if the tmux window's process is still running (pane PID alive), not just whether an OpenCode session exists. Or at minimum, only clean windows for opencode-mode agents.
repro: 1. Spawn agent with --mode claude --tmux (creates tmux window running Claude Code CLI). 2. OpenCode server restarts (e.g. from orch complete rebuild). 3. Run orch clean --sessions. 4. Clean finds 0 active OpenCode sessions, classifies all tmux windows as stale, closes them — killing the active Claude Code agent. Evidence: pw-work-probe-oshcut-shipping-27feb-3a39 was actively running in price-watch and was killed by orch clean.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: orch clean just killed an actively running price-watch agent mid-work. This is data loss — agent progress destroyed by our own tooling. Can't trust orch clean while agents are running.

Scope says ONLY modify clean_cmd.go + tests, but the tmux utility function is directly needed by the fix and is additive-only (no breaking changes). Proceeding since this is minimal supporting infrastructure.

Added PaneProcessChecker with dual-signal liveness detection (pane_current_command + pgrep child processes) as last-resort safety net before classifying windows as stale. Smoke test: orch clean --sessions --dry-run protects active windows. No discovered work.

Window @662 with active claude child process (PID 81396) was correctly protected by the process liveness check.

title: Audit entropy spiral for recoverable features by functional area
description:
## Context
Reverted orch-go to Jan 18 baseline (0bca3dec) after entropy spiral (1163 agent commits, 5.4M LOC churn). Evidence preserved on entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch. Some features built during the spiral were genuinely valuable and should be recovered.

## Approach
Diff Jan 18 (0bca3dec) vs entropy-spiral-feb2026 by functional area. For each area, identify:
- Net-new features (not just churn/refactor cycles)
- Whether the feature compiled/worked at HEAD of spiral branch
- Dependencies on other spiral-era code
- Recovery complexity (clean extract vs needs adaptation)

## Areas to audit
1. **Web UI** - /work-graph route, dashboard agent cards, vite config changes
2. **Spawn system** - Backend abstraction (pkg/spawn/backends/), new flags, spawn pipeline

title: Add CLAUDE.md accretion boundaries section
Add dep-chain grouping mode to cluster connected issues together
description:
## Context

Related issues (e.g., verifiability-first Phase 1→5) are scattered across the tree, interleaved with unrelated work. The existing grouping modes (priority, area, effort) don't address this.

## Task

Add a 'dep-chain' grouping mode that clusters connected issues:
1. Add 'dep-chain' to GroupByMode type in work-graph.ts
2. Implement clusterByDepChain() function:
   - Build adjacency graph from edges (blocks + parent-child)
   - Find connected components via BFS/DFS
   - Name each cluster by the root node's title (or first node alphabetically)
3. Add to GroupByDropdown options
4. Add to 'G' keyboard shortcut cycle
5. Within each cluster, sort by dependency order (leaf→root, or topological)

## Algorithm sketch
```typescript
function clusterByDepChain(nodes: TreeNode[], edges: GraphEdge[]): GroupSection[] {
  // Build adjacency list from edges
  // BFS from each unvisited node to find connected component
  // Return GroupSection per component, named by root issue
}
```

## Dependencies
- Depends on orch-go-pcyb (/api/beads/graph must provide edges)

## Files to modify
- web/src/lib/stores/work-graph.ts - add dep-chain to GroupByMode, implement clusterByDepChain
- web/src/lib/components/group-by-dropdown - add dep-chain option
- web/src/routes/work-graph/+page.svelte - add to groupOrder cycle

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 'dep-chain' appears in group dropdown
- [ ] Connected issues cluster into named sections
- [ ] Isolated issues (no deps) appear in 'Independent' section
- [ ] 'G' keyboard shortcut cycles through all modes including dep-chain
- [ ] npm run build succeeds

title: orch tree: health smell detection with warning badges on clusters
description:
Make orch tree a diagnostic tool by adding health smell detection to clusters.

CONTEXT: orch tree currently renders clusters but gives no signal about which ones need attention. Health smells make the tree a triage tool — the orchestrator sees at a glance where synthesis is needed, where decisions are unacted, where models are untested.

SCOPE:

3. Add smell counts to depth-2 cluster summary line:
   ├─◉ coaching-plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis
4. Smells should also appear in --format json output

├─◉ Coaching Plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis

title: Fix daemon duplicate spawn: issue spawned 10 times in 20 minutes while orchestrator was present
description:
LIVE INCIDENT (Feb 14): orch-go-w50 was spawned 10 times by the daemon in ~20 minutes. All 10 agents did the same work. Issue remained OPEN status with triage:ready label, so the daemon kept picking it up every poll cycle.

Evidence (from orch status --all):
  orch-go-rpj   Extract rework expla...   Complete     4m
  orch-go-12a   Extract rework expla...   Complete     29m
  orch-go-0b1   Extract rework expla...   Planning     1m  (still running at time of detection)
  orch-go-7q8   Extract rework expla...   Complete     18m
  orch-go-vxh   Extract rework expla...   Complete     10m
  orch-go-og3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     23m
  orch-go-bga   Extract rework expla...   Complete     7m
  orch-go-es6   Extract rework expla...   Complete     13m
  orch-go-as3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     25m
  orch-go-c91   Extract rework expla...   Complete     20m

Root cause: daemon dedup cache (TTL-based) is not preventing respawns. The daemon polls bd ready, sees orch-go-w50 is still open+triage:ready, and spawns again. Each spawn gets a unique workspace name so dedup doesnt match.

Cost: ~80K tokens wasted (10 agents x ~8K avg), ~2-3% weekly API budget burned for nothing.

Immediate fix applied: bd update orch-go-w50 --status in_progress (stops daemon from picking it up).

Required fix: daemon must track which beads IDs it has already spawned agents for and refuse to spawn duplicates. The SpawnedIssueTracker already exists but clearly isnt working. This is the same bug documented in .kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/probes/2026-02-14-daemon-duplicate-spawn-ttl-fragility.md

This is P0 because it actively burns API budget and creates confusion every time the daemon runs.

title: Extract spawn gates from spawn_cmd.go to pkg/spawn/gates/
description:
## Goal

Strangler fig extraction: pull 3 gate concerns out of spawn_cmd.go (2,332 lines) into pkg/spawn/gates/. This creates attractor packages so future agents naturally route spawn-gate code there instead of spawn_cmd.go.

## What to extract

1. **hotspot.go** — hotspot analysis/checking logic (currently inline in spawn_cmd.go)
2. **concurrency.go** — concurrency limit checking, capacity management
3. **triage.go** — triage bypass logic, label checking

## Pattern

For each concern:
1. Create pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}.go with the extracted logic
2. spawn_cmd.go calls gates.CheckHotspot(), gates.CheckConcurrency(), etc.
3. Tests move/create in pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}_test.go

## Constraints

- Do NOT refactor spawn_cmd.go beyond what's needed for extraction
- Do NOT change behavior — pure structural extraction
- Each extracted file should be focused and small (<200 lines)
- spawn_cmd.go should shrink by the extracted amount

## Verification

- go build ./cmd/orch/ passes
- go test ./cmd/orch/... passes
- go test ./pkg/spawn/gates/... passes
- spawn_cmd.go line count decreased

title: Implement orch tree CLI MVP (Phase 1): knowledge view + work view
description:
Implement Phase 1 of the knowledge lineage tree visualization as CLI commands.

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-14-knowledge-lineage-tree-visualization.md

SCOPE (Phase 1 only):
1. Parse .kb/ artifacts to extract relationships:
   - Prior-Work tables in investigations
   - Evidence sections in decisions
   - Filesystem hierarchy for models/probes
   - Synthesized From headers in models
2. Implement filesystem-based cluster detection (.kb/investigations/synthesized/{cluster}/)
3. Build ASCII tree renderer (Unicode box-drawing, matching example format in design doc)
4. Two views:
   - orch tree -- knowledge view (investigations/decisions/models as primary nodes, issues as outcomes)
   - orch tree --work -- work view (issues grouped by state as primary nodes, knowledge artifacts as provenance)
5. Basic options: --cluster <name>, --depth <n>, --format json|text

VISUAL FORMAT (must match this exactly):
orch-go knowledge tree
│
├─◉ Entropy Spiral
│ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
│ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
│ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
│ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS

ICON LEGEND:
◉ Investigation (complete)  ◇ Investigation (triage)  ● Issue  ★ Decision  ◆ Model  ◈ Guide

NOT IN SCOPE (Phase 2+):
- Health smell detection (15+ investigations warning)
- Color coding
- Dashboard web integration
- Verification flow integration
- Embedding-based clustering

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree renders full knowledge tree from current .kb/ in <2 seconds
- orch tree --work renders work view grouped by issue state
- orch tree --cluster "entropy-spiral" filters to single cluster
- orch tree --format json outputs valid JSON
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

REFERENCES:
- Design doc: docs/designs/2026-02-14-knowledge-lineage-tree-visualization.md
- Design addendum: Two-view model (line 521+)
- Prior architect issue: orch-go-8f7 (closed)

title: orch clean --sessions deletes active TUI/interactive sessions idle >5min
description:
Problem: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:408-539 deletes sessions not tracked by any .orch/workspace/*/session_id file. TUI/interactive sessions have no workspace, so they bypass Layer 1 protection entirely. If user hasnt sent a message in >5min, Layer 2 (recency) marks session as orphaned and deletes it.

Code path: orch clean --sessions -> cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() -> !trackedSessionIDs[session.ID] (TUI has no workspace, always true) -> now.Sub(updatedAt) > 5min (user paused, true) -> skips IsSessionProcessing (only checked for recent sessions) -> client.DeleteSession (SESSION DELETED).

Fix: Call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions before deletion, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. Minimal fix using existing Layer 3 defense thats currently short-circuited.

Evidence: Model .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #2). Investigation .kb/investigations/2026-02-13-inv-verify-whether-orch-clean-kills-headless-sessions.md. Primary source: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go:464-485.

Files: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go lines 408-539.

title: Agents in other projects fail trying to create cross-repo beads issues
description: Problem: Agents running in project A (e.g., price-watch) discover issues that belong in project B (e.g., orch-go) and try to run 'bd create' with a different workdir. This fails with 'ENOENT: posix_spawn /bin/zsh' because the agent's shell sandbox can't change to another project directory. This is recurring — any agent that discovers a systemic/orchestration issue hits this. Current workaround: agent dumps the bd create command for the human/orchestrator to run manually, but this is fragile and loses issues. Solution options: 1) Add explicit guidance in orchestrator skill and worker-base skill for cross-repo issue creation — agent should output a structured 'CROSS_REPO_ISSUE' block that the orchestrator can pick up and create. 2) Add 'bd create --repo orch-go' support so agents can create issues in other repos without changing workdir. 3) At minimum, update worker skill guidance so agents don't waste attempts and instead cleanly hand off the issue details. Evidence: price-watch orchestrator failed trying to create orch-go-ga6 (push batching issue). Pattern is recurring per user report.

title: Architect: Design accretion gravity enforcement infrastructure
description:
## Problem

Accretion Gravity is a proven principle (spawn_cmd.go grew from reasonable to 2,332 lines as 25+ agents each added features where similar code existed), but it has ZERO enforcement infrastructure. It's a reminder, not a gate — violating Gate Over Remind.

The principle identifies structural constraints as the fix:
- Explicit boundaries in CLAUDE.md ("new logic goes in pkg/, not cmd/")
- File-level rules ("if adding >10 lines to X, create a new package")
- Architectural review at completion, not just functional review

But none of these exist as infrastructure yet. Agents still freely accrete.

## What Needs Designing

Design the enforcement mechanisms that make Accretion Gravity a gate, not a reminder. Consider:

1. **Detection**: How do we detect accretion in real-time (not post-mortem)?
   - File size thresholds in completion verification?
   - Line-count delta checks in pre-commit hooks?
   - Hotspot analysis already exists but only warns

2. **Prevention**: How do we prevent accretion before it happens?
   - CLAUDE.md boundaries that agents actually respect?
   - Spawn context that includes "DO NOT add to these files" directives?
   - File-level extraction rules?

3. **Enforcement**: Where do gates live?
   - Completion verification (orch complete)?
   - Pre-commit hooks?
   - Coaching plugin real-time detection?
   - Spawn-time directives?

4. **Integration**: How does this compose with existing infrastructure?
   - Hotspot analysis (already detects bloated files)
   - Coaching plugin (already injects real-time feedback)
   - Completion verification (already gates on deliverables)
   - Code extraction patterns guide (already documents how to extract)

## Deliverable

Investigation with concrete design recommendations. NOT implementation — just the architecture of enforcement.

## Evidence

- spawn_cmd.go: 2,332 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- session.go: 2,166 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- doctor.go: 1,912 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- coaching.ts: 1,830 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- Principle provenance: .kb/decisions/2026-02-12-strangler-fig-refactor-cmd-orch.md
- Existing guide: .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md

title: Daemon status readers report 'running' from stale file after daemon death
description: handleDaemon() in serve_system.go:419 and readDaemonStatus() in status_cmd.go:1308 report daemon as running based on stale daemon-status.json file existence. After unclean daemon shutdown, the file persists and status is falsely reported as running. Fix: check PID liveness via daemon.pid or apply DetermineStatus() staleness threshold in reader path.
repro: 1. Start daemon: orch daemon run. 2. Kill it: kill -9 PID. 3. Check status: orch status shows 'Daemon - running' because daemon-status.json persists. serve_system.go:419 sets Running=true from file existence, no PID liveness check.

title: Implement keyword coverage multiplier in kb-cli scoring
description:
Root cause fix for OR-matching noise in kb context results. Single keyword hits (e.g., "dashboard" matching 1 of 5 query keywords) score identically to full matches (5/5 keywords). Four prior patches (stop words, frame enrichment, MinStemmedScore, raised threshold) address symptoms but not root cause.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: kb context floods results with irrelevant single-keyword matches. This causes noisy spawn context (66KB of irrelevant knowledge) AND noisy orient decision surfacing. The scoring model needs a keyword coverage dimension so that partial matches rank below full matches.

Implementation: Add keyword coverage multiplier to searchFileContentsWithScore in kb-cli. finalScore = rawScore * max(matchedKeywords/totalKeywords, 0.3). Add CountMatchedKeywords helper to matcher.go.

Target files (kb-cli repo):
- internal/search/matcher.go — Add CountMatchedKeywords
- cmd/kb/search.go:345-421 — Add coverage calculation in searchFileContentsWithScore
- cmd/kb/search_test.go — Coverage scoring tests

Acceptance criteria:
- Query "pricing kpi toolshed dashboard metrics" against file with only "dashboard" in title: score < 3.0 (was 10+)
- Same query against file with all 5 keywords: score ~10.0 (unchanged)
- Single-keyword query "dashboard": score unchanged (coverage = 1.0)
- Existing kb-cli tests pass

Design reference: .kb/investigations/2026-02-27-design-fix-kb-context-query-derivation.md

title: Decision record: verifiability-first as hard operating constraint with explain-back gate
description: Formalize the operating model: (1) System cannot proceed to next deliverable until Dylan has verified the current one. (2) Verification means explain-back in own words, not just approval. (3) This is a hard constraint enforced mechanically, not a recommendation in a skill doc. Should synthesize from existing model at ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/verifiability-first-development.md and the decisions captured today (kb-158420, kb-320fb2). Deliverable: .kb/decisions/ record that becomes the authoritative constraint. Must reference the entropy spiral history as evidence for why this is non-negotiable.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION CONTEXT (Feb 14):

Key finding from session: orch-go has NEVER operated in non-spiral mode. From day 1 (Dec 19), velocity was 40-130 commits/day. The three 'spirals' werent discrete events - they were one continuous acceleration with two rollbacks that reset the code but not the operating pattern. Recovery periods immediately resumed at spiral velocity.

This means verifiability-first isnt preventing a regression - its establishing something that has never existed. The decision record must frame it as: this is the founding operating constraint, not a fix for a bug.

Core constraint: system cannot proceed to next deliverable until Dylan has verified current one through two-gate process (comprehension + behavioral). This is mechanical, not aspirational.

Dylan's words: 'if this means I need to change, then so be it, i'll change. that's where i'm at.'

title: Audit 800+ uncategorized investigations: recommend archive vs cluster labels
description:
The knowledge tree has ~800 investigations in uncategorized. Most are from the entropy spiral era (pre-rollback work). The tree is not useful until these are either archived or properly clustered.

CONTEXT: orch tree shows 8 named clusters and a massive uncategorized bucket. The uncategorized bucket makes the tree dishonest — it hides the actual shape of knowledge. This audit determines what to do with each investigation.

SCOPE:
1. Scan all investigations NOT in .kb/investigations/synthesized/{cluster}/ directories
2. For each, classify into one of:
   a. ARCHIVE — from deleted/rolled-back work, no longer relevant, belongs in .kb/archive/
   b. CLUSTER — still relevant, recommend which cluster it belongs in (existing or new)
   c. KEEP-UNCATEGORIZED — genuinely standalone, no natural cluster
3. Produce a report with counts: how many archive, how many cluster (by cluster name), how many keep
4. For CLUSTER recommendations, suggest area: labels that should be applied going forward
5. Do NOT move any files — just produce the audit report with recommendations

OUTPUT: Investigation artifact with structured recommendations table.

WHY THIS MATTERS: The knowledge tree is becoming Dylan primary interface for project state. 800 uncategorized items make it noise, not signal. This audit is the prerequisite for the tree being honest.

VERIFICATION:
- Report covers all uncategorized investigations (count matches)
- Each investigation has a clear recommendation (archive/cluster/keep)
- Cluster recommendations use consistent naming
- No files moved (audit only)

title: Add skill-class awareness to test_evidence and synthesis completion gates
description: Probe findings (.kb/models/completion-verification/probes/2026-02-13-friction-gate-inventory-all-subsystems.md) show test_evidence (5.5:1 bypass:fail) and synthesis (5.9:1) are noisy because they fire on investigation/docs-only work where they are structurally inapplicable. 31.7% of ALL bypass events (320/1008) stem from this skill-class blindness pattern. Fix: Make these gates skill-class-aware. (1) test_evidence gate should auto-skip (not require manual --skip-test-evidence) when the skill is investigation, architect, research, codebase-audit, or when all changed files are markdown/docs only. (2) synthesis gate should auto-skip for light tier spawns (already partially handled by tier system but probe shows bypasses still occurring) and for investigation skills (the investigation artifact IS the deliverable, not SYNTHESIS.md). Implementation: The skill name is available in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md and/or the .tier file in the workspace. pkg/verify/check.go already reads workspace tier — extend to also read skill from spawn context. Add skill-class categories: code-producing (feature-impl, systematic-debugging, reliability-testing) vs knowledge-producing (investigation, architect, research, codebase-audit). Code-producing skills get all gates. Knowledge-producing skills skip test_evidence and synthesis. Log auto-skips as verification.auto_skipped events in events.jsonl for observability. Verify: go build ./... passes, go test ./pkg/verify/ passes. Test: spawn an investigation agent, complete it — test_evidence and synthesis should not block. PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

title: Worker friction: Edit tool fails on tab-indented Svelte files due to Read tool line-prefix tab collision
description: Claude Code Edit tool fails when files use tab indentation (common in Svelte). The Read tool outputs lines with a 'line_number\t' prefix. When agents copy what appears to be the correct old_string from Read output, the actual tab characters in the file content collide with the prefix delimiter tab. Edit reports 'String to replace not found'. Workers waste tokens retrying (3 attempts observed), then fall back to cat -vet or Python one-liners. For small files they use Write to rewrite entirely. This is a Claude Code limitation, not orch-go, but we may be able to mitigate via spawn context guidance or skill templates advising workers on tab-indented file handling.
repro: 1. Spawn worker targeting a tab-indented Svelte file (e.g. toolshed +page.svelte). 2. Worker reads file with Read tool. 3. Worker attempts Edit with old_string copied from Read output. 4. Edit fails with 'String to replace not found' due to tab ambiguity. Evidence: toolshed worker og-feat-compute-revenue-risk-27feb reported 3 failed Edit attempts on +page.svelte.

title: Remove stale investigation skill at ~/.claude/skills/src/worker/ (superseded by worker/ path)
VerificationTracker backlog count (7) disagrees with orch review count (1) — tracker may be counting already-closed or phantom agents as unverified
description:
**Problem:** `orch daemon preview` reports 'Verification backlog: 7 unverified completions from previous sessions' and pauses the daemon. But `orch review` shows only 1 pending completion. The remaining 6 are invisible to the review workflow, meaning the operator has no way to clear the backlog through the intended path.

**Impact:** Daemon is paused and can't spawn `triage:ready` work (specifically orch-go-v2dv, the Phase 2 heartbeat issue). The only escape is `orch daemon resume` which bypasses verification entirely — defeating the purpose of the constraint.

**Likely cause:** `SeedFromBacklog()` in `pkg/daemon/verification_tracker.go:109` counts agents that `orch review` filters out (already-closed issues, phantom agents, or agents completed without beads tracking). The two systems use different definitions of 'unverified completion'.

**Expected:** Tracker backlog count should match what `orch review` surfaces — the operator should be able to clear the backlog by completing everything in the review queue.

**Reference:** This is a verifiability-first enforcement gap — the gate fires correctly but the release mechanism is broken.

title: Rework daemon: no auto-close, faster polling, review-queue model
description:
Two changes to daemon behavior based on verifiability-first design session:

1. DAEMON NEVER AUTO-CLOSES ISSUES
Current: daemon polls for Phase:Complete, verifies artifacts, closes issue automatically.
New: daemon detects Phase:Complete, flags agent as 'ready for review', but does NOT close the issue. Dylan closes issues through the orchestrator after two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral). Daemon pauses spawning when N agents are waiting for review (default 3) so work doesnt pile up unboundedly.

This means the daemon loop becomes:
  - Poll bd ready, spawn within capacity
  - Monitor for Phase:Complete
  - When agents complete, mark as ready-for-review (not closed)
  - Pause spawning when N agents waiting for review
  - Dylan reviews batch through orchestrator, closes each
  - Daemon sees capacity freed, spawns more

2. FASTER POLL INTERVAL
Current: 60s poll interval feels sluggish. Dashboard doesnt update quickly after CLI commands.
New: Drop poll interval to 15-20s. The daemon is just doing lightweight beads queries - this is not a meaningful load increase. Also reduce spawn delay from 10s to 3-5s (dedup cache already prevents duplicates).

Why both together: the faster polling makes the system feel responsive when Dylan is actively working. The no-auto-close ensures faster polling doesnt mean faster unverified closures.

REMOVES: daemon auto-completion loop (or repurpose it to only flag ready-for-review, not close)
KEEPS: VerificationTracker pause logic (but now its 'pause after N ready-for-review' not 'pause after N auto-completions')
KEEPS: orch daemon resume command

Prior work: 85a0e021 (verification tracker), design session Feb 14 (verifiability-first constraint)
Supersedes the auto-completion behavior from the current daemon.go completion loop.

title: Update skill system for verifiability-first: explain-back scaffolding and human_verified gate
description:
Update the orchestrator skill (skills/src/meta/orchestrator/) to implement completion review as the primary orchestrator workflow.

## What Completion Review Is

Completion review is the two-gate process Dylan uses to engage with agent output. Every completed agent goes through this before closing. This is the orchestrator's primary job — not dispatching work, but understanding what was done.

### Gate 1: Comprehension (Explain-Back)

The orchestrator presents 'the what' — factual scaffolding about what was built/found/decided, in plain language. Dylan explains 'the so-what' — why it matters, what it changes, what concerns him.

Design principles:
- Safe to be wrong (Dylan should feel comfortable saying 'I don't get it')
- Not starting from nothing (orchestrator provides the what)
- Not given the answer (orchestrator does NOT present the so-what)
- No sycophancy: do NOT mirror Dylan's words back, do NOT validate incomplete understanding, do NOT say 'great explanation' when the answer is partial
- If Dylan's explanation misses something important, say so directly
- If Dylan asks a question that reveals genuine understanding, that counts

### Gate 2: Behavioral (Tiered by Work Type)

After comprehension passes:

| Type | Behavioral Gate |
|------|----------------|
| Features | Run it, see it work |
| Bug fixes | Reproduce bug, confirm fixed |  
| Investigations | Spot-check one claim against reality |
| Decisions | N/A (comprehension IS the gate) |

### Pacing

Not every completion review is heavy. Friction scales with complexity/risk:
- Light completions (typo, config, formatting): fast explain-back, minimal behavioral
- Medium completions (single feature, known pattern): standard explain-back, run it
- Heavy completions (architectural change, new subsystem, design decision): thorough explain-back, careful behavioral verification

### Anti-Patterns to Prevent

- Rubber-stamping: orchestrator accepting 'yeah that makes sense' without probing
- Sycophancy: 'Great insight!' when Dylan gives a surface-level answer  
- Ceremony theater: going through the motions on trivial completions
- Skipping comprehension on investigations/decisions because 'artifact is deliverable'
- Presenting options then asking 'which would you pick?' (that's giving the answer)

### Integration Points

- The orchestrator skill's SYNTHESIZE section needs to be rewritten around completion review
- The 'Investigation Outcomes' section needs to include spot-check behavioral gate
- The autonomy section needs to clarify: orchestrator acts autonomously on dispatching, but NEVER skips completion review
- orch complete CLI: --explain flag accepts the explain-back summary (conversational, not CLI prompts)

### What to Modify

Primary file: the orchestrator skill source in skills/src/meta/orchestrator/
Specifically the SYNTHESIZE section, completion review protocol, and investigation outcomes.

Also update worker-base shared skill to require plain-language summary in SYNTHESIS.md (this becomes the scaffolding for explain-back).

### References

- kb-158420 (human verification required)
- kb-320fb2 (explain-back gate) 
- kb-afa611 (sycophancy constraint)
- kb-d4e04d (explain-back is conversational not CLI)
- kb-286915 (completion review model: all types get comprehension gate)
- kb-853ff6 (fail-fast constraint on daemon spawn prerequisites)
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md

title: Redesign control plane circuit breaker heuristics - daily commit count is wrong signal
description: The circuit breaker fired on its first day (59/20 commits) during a normal batch review session. The threshold of 20 commits/day is too aggressive for normal operation (3-4 agents produce this easily). The entropy spiral was 45 commits/day sustained for 26 days — the signal isn't 'too many commits today' but 'too many commits sustained over multiple days without human verification.' The heuristic needs to detect sustained unverified velocity, not single-day spikes. Consider: rolling window (3-day average), human-verified vs agent-only commit distinction, commits-without-human-interaction metric, or graduated warnings before halt. Also: the post-commit hook counts ALL commits including orchestrator cherry-picks from agent workspaces, inflating the count.

description:

   - Pattern: | .kb/investigations/...md | extends | yes | None |

2. Parse Evidence sections in decisions to link decisions under their source investigations

3. Parse Synthesized From headers in models to link models under source investigations
4. Link beads issues to decisions/investigations by scanning issue descriptions for .kb/ path references

6. Handle cycles gracefully (investigations can reference each other)

title: Reconcile model creation threshold: README.md says 3+ but lifecycle guide says 15+ (direct contradiction)
Daemon behavior change: pause after N completions without human verification
description: Current daemon behavior: polls bd ready, auto-spawns indefinitely. This produced the 26-day spiral (1163 commits, 24/7 autonomous operation, zero human involvement). New behavior: daemon tracks completions since last human verification. After N completions without human activity, daemon PAUSES and waits. Not alerts - actually stops spawning. Dylan explicitly resumes when he is back and has reviewed completed work. This is the mechanical enforcement of the verifiability-first constraint. Design considerations: (1) What counts as human activity? Explain-back in orch complete conversation, not just a CLI flag. (2) What is N? Start conservative (e.g., 3 completions). (3) Daemon should surface what is waiting for review when Dylan returns. Prior art: every post-mortem recommended this, none implemented it.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION CONTEXT (Feb 14):

Dylan's insight: daemon pause isn't just safety - it's a workflow design tool. Batching completions into review sessions means: sit down, review N things with two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral), release next batch, walk away. Sustainable rhythm, not constant availability.

Connects to two-gate verification (orch-go-tyi): daemon pauses after N completions. When Dylan returns, he reviews each completion through comprehension gate (what/so-what) then behavioral gate (tiered). Only after verification does system proceed.

Dylan accepts the slowdown if it means quality and understanding.

title: Investigate: which skills produce investigation artifacts and should they?
description:
## Problem

936 investigations exist, only 3 synthesized (312:1 ratio). 45 created in February alone. Investigation production far outpaces synthesis capacity.

Hypothesis: too many skills are configured to produce .kb/investigations/ artifacts when they shouldn't need to. Investigation files should be reserved for genuine exploratory/understanding work, not as a default output for every skill.

## What to check

1. Audit all skill definitions in ~/.claude/skills/ — which ones list investigation artifacts as outputs/deliverables?
2. For each skill that produces investigations: is that appropriate, or should the skill produce a different artifact (or none)?
3. Check skill.yaml or SKILL.md `outputs.required` fields across all skills
4. Check spawn context generation — does it default to investigation deliverables?

## Also check

- Are agents creating investigations opportunistically even when not asked to? (e.g., feature-impl agents creating investigations as side effects)
- Is the investigation template/path being injected into spawn context unnecessarily?

## Deliverable

List of skills that produce investigations, recommendation for which should stop, and what they should produce instead (if anything).

## Evidence

- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 72 archived
- 45 investigations in February 2026 alone
- kb reflect shows 29 synthesis opportunities across 12+ clusters

title: Design: rethink orch spawn flag semantics (model/backend/mode conflation)
description:
## Revised Design: orch spawn flag simplification

### Core Insight
Model and backend are fully independent axes. The opus→claude coupling was a false constraint — opus works through OpenCode API. The --opus flag has no reason to exist.

### Final Design (from completion review)

**Two independent axes:**
1. **Model** (`--model`): what LLM to use. Aliases resolved via config (`~/.orch/config.yaml`).
2. **Backend** (`--backend`): how to run the session (opencode API / claude CLI).

**Visibility:** `--tmux` is the only opt-in. Default is headless. `--inline` and `--attach` removed.

**Config-based model aliases:**
```yaml
models:
  opus: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
  sonnet: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929
  flash: google/gemini-3-flash-preview
```

**Removed:**
- `--opus` flag (just use `--model opus`)
- `--inline` flag (never used)
- `--attach` flag (just use tmux directly)
- model→backend auto-selection (false constraint)

**Fixed:**
- Infrastructure detection: advisory only, explicit `--backend` always wins
- `--mode` validation: reject 'claude'/'opencode' values with helpful error
- Default backend: opencode (matches daemon common case)

### Implementation issues
- orch-go-XXXX: Phase 1 (non-breaking fixes)
- orch-go-XXXX: Phase 2 (flag removal + config aliases)

### Reference
- Design synthesis: .orch/workspace/og-work-rethink-orch-spawn-15feb-d93f/SYNTHESIS.md
- False constraint: kb-0b3f89 (opus does NOT require claude backend)

title: Probe: do agents naturally route to extracted packages without being told?
description:
## Hypothesis

After extracting spawn gates to pkg/spawn/gates/, agents given spawn-gate-related tasks will naturally find and add code to the extracted packages — NOT to spawn_cmd.go — without being told where to put it.

## Protocol

1. Wait for extraction (orch-go extract spawn gates issue) to complete
2. Spawn a feature-impl agent with a task like "add rate-limit gate to spawn" or "add file-size pre-check to spawn"
3. CRITICAL: Do NOT mention pkg/spawn/gates/ in the spawn context. Let the agent discover the codebase.
4. After completion, check: where did the agent add code?

## Success criteria

- Agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ and added code there → CONFIRMS hypothesis
- Agent added to spawn_cmd.go anyway → CONTRADICTS hypothesis, need stronger signals

## What to record

- Which files the agent created/modified (git diff)
- Whether the agent mentioned discovering pkg/spawn/gates/ in its reasoning
- Total lines added to spawn_cmd.go vs pkg/spawn/gates/

## Constraint

This is an ORCHESTRATOR probe — the orchestrator runs this manually after extraction completes. Do NOT spawn this as autonomous work. The value is in observing agent behavior, not automating the observation.

title: Architect: Knowledge lineage tree visualization for dashboard and orchestrator
description:
Design a tree-based knowledge lineage visualization that shows the natural parent-child relationships between knowledge artifacts (investigations, decisions, models, probes, guides) and the beads issues they spawn.

The tree structure falls out naturally from existing data:
- Investigations reference prior work (Prior-Work table)
- Decisions cite investigations (Evidence section)
- Issues have descriptions pointing to decisions
- Probes live inside model directories (filesystem hierarchy)
- Models synthesize from investigations (Synthesized From)

Example rendering (Unicode box-drawing tree):

orch-go knowledge tree
│
├─◉ Entropy Spiral
│ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-12-inv-entropy-spiral-postmortem.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
│ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions   CLOSED
│ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
│ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS
│ └─◉ handoffs/2026-02-13-entropy-spiral-recovery.md

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. Where does this render? Dashboard (Svelte, persistent, browsable), orchestrator session (inline, contextual during verification), or both?
2. Data model: How to extract the tree from existing artifacts? Parse Prior-Work tables, Synthesized From headers, beads deps, filesystem hierarchy? Or add explicit lineage metadata?
3. Interaction: Click to expand/collapse? Filter by area? Highlight verification state (verified vs pending)? Show health smells (15 investigations with no decision = cluster smell)?
4. Integration with verify flow: When daemon pauses and Dylan reviews, show the tree with new nodes highlighted so he sees where work fits before explain-back begins.
5. CLI vs web: Should 'orch tree' or 'kb tree' exist as CLI command (for orchestrator session rendering) in addition to dashboard?
6. Grouping: The top-level clusters (Entropy Spiral, Coaching Plugin, etc.) - are these inferred from area: labels, filesystem paths, or explicit topic tags?

USE CASES:
- Session start: see the knowledge landscape, find where to focus
- Triage: spot clusters with many investigations but no decisions (smell)
- Verification: see where a completed deliverable fits before explain-back
- Orientation: new session Claude sees the tree, immediately understands project structure

CONSTRAINTS:
- Must work with existing .kb/ file structure and beads issue data
- Tree relationships already implicit in artifacts - design should extract, not require manual curation
- Dylan doesnt read Go - the tree IS his primary interface for understanding project state

Prior art: The tree rendering above was produced in conversation and Dylan said 'wow, this is a really cool view' - that specific format resonated.

title: Daemon verification tracker reads labels instead of checkpoint file — enforcement gap
description:
PROBLEM: orch daemon run --dry-run reports 'Verification check: 0/3 unverified completions' despite verification-checkpoints.jsonl containing 13 entries with gate2_complete:false. The verification pause gate — the flagship control-plane mechanism from the verifiability-first decision — is not firing.

ROOT CAUSE: SeedFromBacklog() counts issues with daemon:ready-review label (via issue_adapter.go:168). When issues are closed via orch complete, the label is removed. So closed-but-unverified completions disappear from the daemon's view. The checkpoint file persists the unverified state correctly, but nothing reads it for enforcement.

This is a composition bug: checkpoint writing works (gate1/gate2 recorded correctly), label-based counting works (counts labels accurately), but they measure different things. Labels track active review queue. Checkpoints track verification state. The daemon uses the wrong one for enforcement.

FIX: The daemon's verification check should read verification-checkpoints.jsonl directly and count entries where gate2_complete is false (for Tier 1 work) or gate1_complete is false (for Tier 2 work). SeedFromBacklog should be replaced or updated to use the checkpoint file as source of truth, not labels.

VERIFICATION: After fix, orch daemon run --dry-run should report 13/3 unverified completions (or whatever the real count is) and refuse to spawn. The checkpoint file IS the source of truth — labels are the view layer.

EVIDENCE: Observed during bootstrap verification sequence (orch-go-hi8g). This confirms the bootstrap model prediction that gates may exist but not fire.

Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Constraint 3: Mechanical Enforcement)

title: Audit daemon for warn-and-continue anti-pattern; establish fail-fast as structural constraint
description: The duplicate spawn incident (orch-go-2ma) was caused by a 'log warning and continue' pattern in the spawn flow. This pattern likely exists elsewhere in daemon code. Two parts: (1) audit daemon.go for other warn-and-continue paths that should be fail-fast, (2) establish a kb constraint that spawn prerequisites are hard gates, not soft warnings. Goal: make fail-fast structural so the next person writing daemon code defaults to it.

title: [orch-go] ux-audit: Audit the orch-go dashboard UX across both view...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Audit the orch-go dashboard UX across both views: http://localhost:5188/ (main dashboard) and http://localhost:5188/work-graph (work graph). ORIENTATION_FRAME: Dylan's dashboard split into two views and now neither gives the full operational picture. The daemon pauses with '10 unverified completions' and he can't tell which projects they're in. He's ready to remove the main dashboard view but it still has useful things work-graph lacks. CRITICAL EVALUATION CRITERION: The dashboard must be a LIVE OPERATIONAL VIEW showing real-time worker lifecycle events (spawn, phase transitions, completion, errors) without manual refresh. This is the #1 requirement — if a view doesn't show timely/recent agent lifecycle information, it fails regardless of other merits. Audit scope: (1) Main dashboard — what's useful that work-graph doesn't cover? (2) Work-graph — what works well, what's missing? (3) Gap analysis — what does an operator need at a glance? Agent status, completions needing review, cross-project visibility, triage queue, live lifecycle events. (4) Recommendation — consolidate or keep two? What gets cut, merged, added?

title: [orch-go] investigation: Worker detection stress test - do many tool cal...
Guard bd sync against bulk file commits
description: bd sync does a bulk git add + commit that sweeps in whatever state the worktree is in, with no awareness of what SHOULD be committed. On a clean worktree this is fine (Jan 18 bd syncs only touched .beads/ and .kb/quick/), but in a dirty worktree it becomes destructive. Fix: bd sync should limit its git add scope to .beads/ and .kb/quick/ files only, never git add -A. Or add a safety check: if staged changes touch >N files or include non-beads paths, abort with a warning.

Epic: Make issue dependency structure visible in dashboard (effective priority + topological ordering)
description:
## Understanding

Problem: Issue dependency structure (blocking chains, effective priority, critical path) exists in beads but gets lost in the API pipeline. Dashboard renders flat/alphabetical lists, hiding the structural information users need to prioritize work. Previous approaches: work-graph shows deps as edges but doesn't compute effective priority or topological ordering. Key constraints: beads computeLayout() already solves topological layering but only for single-issue display, not export. Frontend GraphNode.layer field exists but is never populated. Risk: cross-repo changes to bd export format could break other consumers. Done: dashboard sorts by effective priority, shows badge when declared!=effective, topological layer ordering reveals critical path.

---

Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-inv-design-issue-structure-visibility.md. Phase 1: orch-go API enrichment. Phase 2: dashboard rendering. Phase 3 (deferred): bd ready --sort=effective.

description: Port beads computeLayout() (graph.go:322-418, longest-path topological layering) to orch-go pkg/graph/. Add effective priority computation: transitive closure where P2 blocking P1 blocking P0 surfaces as eff:P0. Ref: .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-inv-design-issue-structure-visibility.md

Extract spawn flags phase 1: --mode from cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go into pkg/orch/. Pure structural extraction — no behavior changes.
Extraction logic lacks convergence: re-creates extraction for files still above threshold after prior extraction
description: The daemon extraction logic (pkg/daemon/extraction.go) has no memory of prior extractions. CheckExtractionNeeded() runs on every issue every poll cycle. If the target file remains above 1500 lines after extraction, it creates a new extraction issue each time the parent becomes unblocked. Evidence: 9 duplicate extraction issues for spawn_cmd.go from orch-go-hcrg. Fix: track (parent_issue, critical_file) pairs that have already been extracted.

title: Extraction issues recursively trigger more extraction via parseable file paths in titles
description: GenerateExtractionTask() produces titles containing the critical file path. When daemon processes these extraction issues, InferTargetFilesFromIssue() parses the file path from the title, triggering recursive extraction. inferConcernFromIssue() strips the 'extract' prefix, creating progressively longer concatenated titles. Fix: either exclude extraction issues from extraction checks (skip titles starting with 'Extract'), or remove file paths from generated titles.

title: Implement work-graph three-layer priority cascade (mutual exclusivity)
description: Implement the design from .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-design-work-graph-issues-view-sections.md. Three-layer priority cascade: Ready to Complete > WIP > Tree. Each issue in exactly ONE section. Pass readyToCompleteIds as excludeIds prop to WorkGraphTree, merge with pinnedTreeIds in flattenVisibleTree. Filter wipItems by excludeIds too. Files: +page.svelte, work-graph-tree.svelte. Acceptance: no issue appears in more than one section.

title: Phase 2: Daemon model-drift reflection with throttled issue creation
description: Phase 2 of knowledge maintenance automation loop (.kb/decisions/2026-02-15-knowledge-maintenance-automation-loop.md). Daemon reads staleness events from ~/.orch/model-staleness-events.jsonl and creates beads issues when spawn-count >= 3 for same model. Add ReflectModelDriftEnabled and ReflectModelDriftInterval (default 4h) to daemon Config. Add RunModelDriftReflection() that: 1) reads staleness events, 2) counts per model, 3) deduplicates against existing open issues, 4) respects backpressure (max 3 open model-maintenance issues), 5) batches related models in same domain. Label created issues: model-maintenance. Priority: P2 for deleted files or >=5 commits changed, P3 for minor changes. Circuit breaker: halt creation if 5+ open model-maintenance issues. Acceptance: daemon creates model-update issue after 3+ stale spawns for same model; stops creating when 3 open.

title: Audit features through ATC lens: what was built for the conductor model?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Audit orch-go features through ATC lens. The ATC-not-conductor reframe (decision: .kb/decisions/2026-02-28-atc-not-conductor-orchestrator-reframe.md) says the orchestrator is Air Traffic Control, not an orchestra conductor. Conductor invests in observation-during-flight (see everything). ATC invests in pre-flight briefing (spawn context) and post-flight debrief (completion to knowledge). Audit the actual orch-go feature set — every command, every subsystem — and categorize: (1) conductor-shaped: built for real-time observation/direction/micro-management, (2) ATC-shaped: built for sequencing, separation, clearance, context injection, (3) under-invested from ATC perspective. For each conductor-shaped feature, assess: is it pure conductor (deprecate candidate), or does it have an ATC function if reframed? For under-invested areas, identify specific gaps. Deliverable: .kb/investigations/2026-02-28-inv-atc-lens-feature-audit.md

System is ~70% ATC-shaped (spawn/gates/daemon/completion), ~20% conductor-shaped (monitoring features), ~10% under-invested ATC (debrief pipeline, file separation, handoff). 3 deprecation candidates (send, monitor, patterns), 4 reframeable features, 4 ATC gaps identified. Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-28-inv-atc-lens-feature-audit.md. No discovered work (follow-up recommendations in SYNTHESIS.md NextActions). Leave it Better: kb-69e152 (orch send conductor finding), kb-13d1f7 (debrief pipeline constraint).

comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
70/20/10 ATC split confirmed. 3 deprecation candidates (monitor, patterns, send — though send needs reframe to emergency response channel, not full deprecation). Biggest gap: debrief-to-knowledge pipeline. System organically ATC-shaped already.

title: Global decisions flood non-orch projects in kb context
description:
ORIENTATION_FRAME: When agents spawn into toolshed or price-watch, they get 20+ global orch-philosophy decisions that eat context budget and provide zero domain value. Same 66KB irrelevant knowledge problem, just from global store instead of local.

Root cause: GetGlobalStoreContext() in kb-cli always merges ~/.kb/ results unconditionally. Global decisions match on common words like "fix", "design", "strategic". The coverage multiplier helps scoring but global results bypass the local-first threshold — they're injected without the same coverage gating.

Fix options:
1. Apply same coverage scoring + minimum threshold to global results
2. Higher inclusion threshold for global results than local
3. Both

Target: kb-cli (GetGlobalStoreContext in search.go or context.go)
Related: orch-go-58ea (coverage multiplier, now landed)
repro: cd ~/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/toolshed && kb context 'fix deployment pipeline' — returns 22 [global] decisions, zero toolshed-specific results

title: Design: daemon singleton enforcement — prevent multiple daemon processes
description: Multiple daemon processes end up running concurrently (observed: PID from Tuesday still running alongside today's). No lock/PID file mechanism prevents this. Causes: duplicate spawns, duplicate skill inference loops, wasted resources. Need singleton enforcement so only one daemon runs at a time.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Design singleton enforcement for the orch daemon — prevent multiple daemon processes from running concurrently.

ORIENTATION_FRAME: Multiple daemon processes keep ending up running at once (just found PID from Tuesday still running alongside today's). This causes duplicate spawn inference loops, wasted resources, and confusing behavior. Need a robust singleton mechanism.

PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

SESSION SCOPE:
- Read current daemon implementation: pkg/daemon/daemon.go, cmd/orch/daemon.go
- Read how the daemon is started (manual 'orch daemon run', nohup, previously attempted launchd)
- Understand current lifecycle: no PID file, no lock, no check for existing process
- Design singleton enforcement that works across all start methods

DELIVERABLES:
- Architectural recommendation for singleton mechanism (PID file, flock, socket, etc.)
- Concrete implementation shape: where the lock lives, what happens on conflict, how stale locks are handled
- Consider: graceful handoff (new daemon replaces old) vs hard rejection (refuse to start)
- Consider: how 'orch daemon restart' should work
- Sequenced implementation issues created via bd create with dependencies

IN SCOPE:
- Singleton enforcement mechanism design
- Stale lock/PID detection (process died without cleanup)
- Integration with existing daemon start paths
- How orch status reports daemon health
- How orch daemon restart/stop should behave

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Actually implementing anything
- Changing the daemon's polling/spawning logic
- Dashboard changes

AUTHORITY: Read-only on all code and skill files. Create beads issues. Write investigation/decision files in .kb/.

PRIOR CONTEXT:
- Two daemons were running: PID 34172 (from Tuesday) and PID 54135 (from today)
- The stale daemon was running the old binary (pre-concurrency fix), causing spawn inference loops that never resolved
- Current start method: 'nohup orch daemon run' or manual foreground
- Previously attempted launchd (com.orch.daemon) but service not currently registered

title: [orch-go] architect: ORIENTATION_FRAME: Gate friction is the biggest...

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ORIENTATION_FRAME: Gate friction is the biggest systemic tax on orchestration throughput. The synthesis gate alone has 107 bypasses, 1925 auto-skips, and a 16.3% fail rate. Light-tier work constantly triggers gates designed for heavy-tier work, requiring bespoke bypass justifications every time. Dylan wants to understand the full gate friction landscape and come out with a redesigned gate architecture that is tier-aware.

SESSION SCOPE: Analyze the current verification gate system in orch-go and design a tier-aligned gate architecture that eliminates unnecessary friction.

IN SCOPE:
- Analyze pkg/verify/check.go for current gate logic and tier handling
- Analyze orch stats output (470 spawns, 183 skip events, gate breakdown data)
- Review the bypass reason patterns from orch stats --verbose
- Review .kb/decisions/ for existing gate-related decisions
- Map which gates apply to which tiers and identify mismatches
- Design a tier-aware gate matrix (light/medium/heavy x gate requirements)
- Identify gates that should be removed, merged, or made tier-conditional
- Propose concrete changes to pkg/verify/

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Implementing the changes (separate feature-impl agents)
- Changing the tier system itself
- Daemon or spawn logic changes

DELIVERABLES:
- SYNTHESIS.md in workspace
- .kb/investigations/ file with full analysis
- Implementation issues created in beads for each recommended change

VERIFICATION: Analysis must reference actual code paths in pkg/verify/, actual stats data, and actual bypass patterns. No speculative recommendations — every proposal must trace to observed friction.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Planning - Analyzing gate friction landscape across pkg/verify/ and orch stats data

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: investigation_path: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/investigations/2026-02-28-design-orientation-frame-gate-friction-biggest.md

title: Exclude non-knowledge files from kb search (a11y snapshots, HTML dumps)
description:
ORIENTATION_FRAME: Raw a11y snapshot files score highest in search results because they contain many domain words in raw page dumps, drowning out actual knowledge artifacts.

Root cause: Large files with many scattered keyword occurrences accumulate score. No file-type or content-length signal in scoring.

Fix options:
1. Exclude known non-knowledge file patterns from search (a11y-snapshot-*, raw HTML captures)
2. Add content-length penalty for very large files
3. Both

Target: kb-cli search paths (searchFileContentsWithScore or file discovery)
repro: cd ~/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/toolshed && kb search 'pricing kpi revenue dashboard' — a11y-snapshot-1280.md scores 69.98, highest result

title: kb search default limit too high (returns 100+ results)
description:
ORIENTATION_FRAME: kb search returns unbounded results (116 for a normal price-watch query, 330 for global). Most consumers apply their own limits but direct kb search callers get flooded.

Symptom: kb search "shipping cost estimation oshcut" returns 116 results from price-watch.

Root cause: --limit flag defaults to 0 (unlimited). kb context applies perCategoryLimits but kb search does not.

Fix: Default --limit to 20-30 for kb search. Preserve --limit 0 for explicit unlimited. Low impact since kb context already handles its own limits.

Target: kb-cli cmd/kb/search.go

title: Fix worker detection: re-implement ORCH_WORKER header → session.metadata.role in OpenCode fork
description: Worker coaching metrics have been broken since inception (0 worker metrics ever recorded vs 1284+ orchestrator metrics). Root cause: server-side code that reads x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER header and sets session.metadata.role='worker' was lost during upstream rebase. The chain: (1) orch spawn sets header x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER=1 ✅ verified in pkg/opencode/client.go. (2) OpenCode server should read header and set session.metadata.role='worker' ❌ MISSING - lost in rebase. (3) Coaching plugin checks session?.metadata?.role === 'worker' ✅ implemented but never fires. The session.metadata schema was added Feb 14 (commit 36f084ca5) so the infrastructure now exists. Fix is ~5-10 lines in packages/opencode/src/session.ts to read the header during session creation and write metadata.role.

If no warning appears, the detection is enforcement theater. Reference: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-design-solution-model-artifact-staleness.md. Related closures: orch-go-bm9 (Phase 1 backfill), orch-go-2qj (Phase 2 detection). title: [orch-go] investigation: Audit orch-go implementation of the model/probe...
Daemon: auto-spawn extraction before feature work on CRITICAL hotspots
description:
## Goal

When daemon processes a triage:ready issue that targets a CRITICAL hotspot file (>1,500 lines), automatically spawn an extraction agent first to create the attractor package, then spawn the feature agent.

## How it works

1. Daemon picks up issue from triage:ready queue
2. Daemon runs hotspot check (already exists) against issue description/title to infer target files
3. If target file is CRITICAL (>1,500 lines): spawn extraction agent first, block feature spawn until extraction completes
4. If target file is fine: spawn feature agent normally

## Extraction agent task

"Extract [inferred concern] from [file] into [pkg/appropriate/package/]. Pure structural extraction — no behavior changes."

The extraction creates the attractor. Then the feature agent discovers it naturally (proven by probe orch-go-8ao: agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ without being told).

## Evidence

- Probe confirmed: agents naturally route to extracted packages (orch-go-8ao)
- spawn_cmd.go shrank from 2,332 → 1,973 lines across two extractions
- Agent even pulled existing inline code into the attractor package

## Constraints

- Don't over-engineer the file targeting — title/description keyword matching is fine for v1
- Extraction agent should be small-scoped (one concern at a time)
- If hotspot detection can't determine target file, skip and spawn normally

title: Prevent duplicate issue creation: title-based dedup on bd create
description:
**Problem:** beads allows creating multiple issues with identical titles. The daemon's extraction path shells out to `bd create` and can create N issues with the same title but different IDs. This is the root cause of 5x duplicate spawns (orch-go-e0o3).

**Current state:** `CreateArgs.Force` exists in the Go client but isn't used by orch-go's extraction path (which uses CLI). No title-based dedup exists at the beads layer.

**Desired behavior:** `bd create` should check for existing open issues with the same title before creating a new one. If a match exists, return the existing issue ID instead of creating a duplicate. A `--force` flag allows explicit override when duplicates are intentional.

**Defense in depth:** orch-go already has a downstream safety net (title-based dedup at spawn time, orch-go-e0o3). This beads-side fix prevents the duplicates from being created in the first place.

**Evidence:** 5 identical issues created: orch-go-l8k2, kzqq, pg9l, cu0r, xy7n — all 'Extract spawn flags phase 1: --mode'. Each spawned a separate agent that found work already done.

title: Phase 1: Add behavioral verification (gate2) recording to orch complete
description: Phase 1 of verifiability-first decision. Currently orch complete --explain writes checkpoint with gate1_complete=true but gate2_complete is ALWAYS false. Every entry in ~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl has gate2_complete:false. Need: 1) orch complete --verified flag (or similar) that records gate2_complete=true after human observes behavior. 2) Tier-aware gating: Tier 1 (features/bugs) requires BOTH gates. Tier 2 (investigations/probes) requires only gate1. Tier 3 (trivial) requires acknowledge only. 3) Tier inference from beads issue type. Without this, the checkpoint file records comprehension but never behavioral verification — half the two-gate system is missing. Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md Phase 1 items 3-5.

Click freeze reactive capture script
description: Build a shell script that captures full system state for click freeze diagnosis. When triggered (manually first, then via Karabiner hotkey), it should dump: process list (pgrep for suspect services), memory_pressure, vm_stat, IOKit HID state (ioreg), WindowServer stats, launchctl print-disabled, and timestamp — all to a log directory (~/.orch/click-freeze-captures/ or similar). Each capture is a timestamped snapshot file. After manual testing, we'll bind it to a Karabiner hotkey in dotfiles. Context: see .kb/models/macos-click-freeze.md for the full investigation. H6 (aggregate service contention) is leading hypothesis but freeze is intermittent — we need correlation data from multiple occurrences rather than elimination testing.

title: Phase 4: Violation handling — logging, orch control status, resume command
description: Phase 4 of verifiability-first decision. When verification constraints are violated, the system needs structured response: 1) Violation log (~/.orch/metrics/verification-violations.log) — records every violation with timestamp, beads_id, constraint violated, reason. 2) orch control status command — shows current verification state: unverified completions, heartbeat age, daemon paused/running, override count. 3) orch control resume --verified 'explanation' — explicit override to resume after violation halt. Requires explanation (logged). 4) Monthly review of override frequency — if >50% use override in any week, constraint is broken, escalate to architecture change. The override path is intentional — for emergencies and false positives. But it requires explicit action and explanation, not passive continuation. Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md Phase 4.

title: Architect: Probe as primary evidence artifact — retire investigations
description: Dylan values the probe concept because it forces specificity: 'test this claim' vs 'look into this area'. Investigations feel like one-offs disconnected from models, while probes create granularity useful for thinking ('what probes do we need before deciding?'). But the current system creates friction: Dylan has to decide 'investigation or probe?' before requesting evidence-gathering, and the routing logic is split across orchestrator skill (spawn-time detection) and investigation skill (mode detection from SPAWN_CONTEXT markers). Design question: should probes become the universal evidence-gathering primitive, with investigations retired? Key constraints: (1) probes currently must target a model — would need to support targeting any claim source (model, decision, assumption, code), (2) probes currently live nested under models (.kb/models/{name}/probes/) — may need their own location for discoverability, (3) investigation skill has significant infrastructure (D.E.K.N., Prior Work tables, self-review) that probes lack, (4) ~800+ existing investigations in .kb/investigations/ need migration/coexistence strategy. Dylan's framing: 'I should be thinking about what I want to understand, not which artifact shape to use.' The routing decision should be invisible to the meta-orchestrator.

title: Fix pre-existing build error: session_resume.go duplicate declarations with session.go
Daemon dedup fundamentally broken: same spawn flags extraction task spawned 5 times (l8k2, kzqq, pg9l, cu0r, xy7n)
description:
**Problem:** The daemon spawned 'Extract spawn flags phase 1: --mode from cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go into pkg/orch/' five separate times as five separate beads issues: orch-go-l8k2, orch-go-kzqq, orch-go-pg9l, orch-go-cu0r, orch-go-xy7n. Each agent found the work was already done and produced no changes.

**Prior fix:** orch-go-dr0u fixed a single duplicate spawn (orch-go-09cc spawned twice). That fix clearly didn't hold — we're now at 5x duplication of the same task.

**Impact:** Wasted agent capacity (5 slots used for 1 task), verification backlog pollution (each dup needs completion review), and operator confusion. With max 5 concurrent agents, 5 duplicates means the entire capacity was consumed by one task.

**Likely cause:** The TTL-based dedup cache doesn't survive daemon restarts, or the cache key doesn't match across issue re-creation. Each duplicate has a slightly different beads ID but identical content.

**Expected:** Daemon should never spawn work that is already in-progress or completed by another agent. Dedup should check: (1) is an agent already working on this issue? (2) is identical work already done (same title/description)?

**Evidence:** Session on Feb 16 — closed l8k2, then kzqq appeared, then pg9l, then cu0r, then xy7n. All five confirmed work already done in commit 41f5a781.

title: Auto-surface orch stats summary at orchestrator session start via session.created event hook
description:
Phase 5 of coaching metrics redesign (see .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-design-coaching-metrics-redesign.md). Depends on Phase 2 (stats has coaching section).

Add event handler in plugins/coaching.ts for session.created:
1. Listen for session.created event via the event hook
2. Check if session is worker (metadata.role) — skip if so
3. Shell out to 'orch stats --days 1 --json' (async, non-blocking)
4. Parse JSON output, format compact health summary
5. Inject via client.session.prompt({ noReply: true })

Run async (child_process.exec not execSync) to avoid blocking session start. ~500ms latency is acceptable.

Skip injection if session.created fires for worker sessions (metadata.role === 'worker').

Key consideration: coaching.ts will be ~1330 lines after Phase 3 removals. This adds ~50-80 lines, keeping it under 1500 accretion boundary.

IMPORTANT: Keep both copies in sync — plugins/coaching.ts AND .opencode/plugin/coaching.ts.

title: Design session: collaborative tree-building as primary work interface
description:
Dylan imagines an interaction model where he and the AI orchestrator collaboratively build out the knowledge/work tree. In this model:

- The tree isnt two separate views (knowledge vs work) — its one living structure
- Nodes transform: issue (work) → completed → knowledge artifact (investigation, decision, model)
- Dylan and orchestrator grow branches together in conversation: 'this area needs a probe', 'this decision should spawn these three things'
- The tree IS the project. Issues are the mechanism, the tree is the interface.
- Lines between knowledge tree and work tree are blended as issues turn into knowledge

This is NOT the same as the knowledge tree visualization (orch-go-8f7). That is 'render what exists.' This is 'a new interaction model for shaping work.'

Key insight from session: in Dylans system, work and knowledge arent separate things. An investigation IS work that BECOMES knowledge. A decision IS knowledge that SPAWNS work. The current separation (beads for work, .kb for knowledge) is an implementation detail, not a real boundary.

Dylan said: 'this deserves its own design session.'

This is triage:review because it needs a real design session with Dylan present — this is about how HE wants to interact with the system, not something an agent can design alone.

title: Daemon may be spawning duplicate agents on in_progress issues, clobbering tmux windows
description: Dylan has observed tmux windows vanishing while agents are mid-work. This is recurring. Hypothesis: daemon doesn't properly detect existing running agents for an issue and spawns a duplicate, which clobbers the tmux window of the original. Need to investigate: (1) Does daemon check for existing agents before spawning? (2) What happens to the tmux window when a second spawn targets the same issue? (3) Check daemon spawn logs for duplicate spawns on same beads ID.
repro: Worker agents running in tmux windows disappear mid-work. Recurring. Suspected cause: daemon spawns new agent on an issue that already has a running agent, new spawn overwrites or closes the existing tmux window.

Investigate: (1) How does the daemon check for existing running agents before spawning? Trace the dedup logic in cmd/orch/daemon.go. (2) What happens in orch spawn when targeting an issue that already has an active workspace/tmux window? Does it overwrite? (3) Check if there's a race between daemon poll cycle detecting 'ready' issues and an agent that's already been spawned but hasn't updated its beads status yet. title: Knowledge maintenance: 57 active quick entries need promotion/pruning
Architect: design language-agnostic accretion metrics for cross-project orchestration
description:
## Problem

Accretion gravity enforcement (file size thresholds, hotspot detection, completion gates) is currently hardcoded for orch-go — a Go project with specific structure. We want these metrics available for ANY project we orchestrate, regardless of language (Go, TypeScript, Python, Rust, Svelte, etc.) or file structure conventions.

## Key Design Questions

1. **Threshold calibration:** Are 800/1500 line thresholds universal, or do they need to be language-relative? (A 1500-line Python file is very different from a 1500-line Go file with verbose error handling. A 200-line Svelte component might be bloated while a 200-line Go file is fine.)

2. **What counts as a 'source file'?** Generated files (.svelte-kit/), test files (*_test.go), config files, vendored deps — how do we filter these language-agnostically?

3. **Project configuration:** How does a project declare its accretion policy? Per-repo config file? Convention-based defaults? Language auto-detection?

4. **Metric collection:** The accretion.delta event in orch complete uses git diff + wc -l — this is language-agnostic already. What else is needed?

5. **Integration with existing infrastructure:** orch hotspot, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching plugin — which parts are already portable vs orch-go specific?

## Evidence (orch-go stats as baseline)

Current orch-go file size distribution (490 source files, 154K total lines):
- 0-100 lines: 141 files (28.8%)
- 100-300 lines: 170 files (34.7%)  — median is 205 lines
- 300-500 lines: 93 files (19.0%)
- 500-800 lines: 42 files (8.6%)
- 800-1500 lines: 31 files (6.3%) — WARNING zone
- 1500+ lines: 13 files (2.7%) — CRITICAL zone

Top offenders: spawn_cmd.go (2173), doctor.go (1909), complete_cmd.go (1847), coaching.ts (1830), status_cmd.go (1625)

## Constraint

This is a design/architecture question. Produce recommendations, not implementation. Consider what a .orch/accretion.yaml or similar config might look like.

## Prior Knowledge

- Current enforcement: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-architect-design-accretion-gravity-enforcement.md (four-layer architecture, hardcoded 800/1500 thresholds)
- kn decision: Accretion gravity fix is structural extraction (strangler fig), not detection/warnings
- Accretion metric already captured: accretion.delta events in orch complete track file-level lines added/removed
